EXO 29:38–29:42; ; EZR 3:3; LEV 8:18–8:21 at the ordination of priests; LEV 16:3, LEV 16:24; NUM 28:11–28:14 on the first of the month, the new moon; NUM 28:19, NUM 28:23; LEV 23:12, LEV 23:18; ; NUM 28:27; NUM 29:2, NUM 29:6; NUM 29:13; LUK 2:24 for purification after childbirth

Burnt offerings in idolatrous worship

EXO 32:6; ; 2KI 10:25

Warnings about misplaced confidence in the burnt offering

1SA 15:22 See also ISA 1:11–1:15; JER 7:21–7:22; HOS 6:6; AMO 5:25; MIC 6:6–6:8

NT references to the burnt offering

Jesus Christ endorsed the prophets' teaching

MRK 12:33–12:34 See also MAT 9:13, MAT 12:7

The holy life of Jesus Christ is the perfect burnt offering

HEB 10:5–10:10 See also HEB 10:14

title: 7324: calf worship
content:
A heathen (notably Egyptian and Canaanite) practice, in which statues of bull calves were worshipped as symbols of fertility and physical strength. Contrary to the second commandment this was copied by the Israelites and by Jeroboam after the division of the kingdom, in both cases incurring God's wrath.

Aaron made a golden calf for the Israelites to worship

This act was born partly of impatience

EXO 32:1–32:4 See also EXO 32:7–32:8; PSA 106:19–106:22; ACT 7:41

It was swiftly condemned

EXO 32:19–32:20 See also EXO 32:35; DEU 9:16–9:21; PSA 106:23

King Jeroboam initiated calf worship

As a matter of expediency

1KI 12:26–12:30 See also 2KI 17:15–17:16; 2CH 11:14–11:15, 2CH 13:8

Jeroboam's sin was one of the main reasons for the Assyrian captivity

2KI 17:18–17:28 See also HOS 8:4–8:9, HOS 10:5–10:6

Calf worship is detestable and useless

DEU 7:25; ; ISA 44:9–44:20, ISA 46:6–46:7; ; ROM 1:21–1:23

title: 7326: cannibalism
content:
God warns his people about cannibalism

DEU 28:53; ; LEV 26:29; ; JER 19:9

Cannibalism as a result of God's judgment on his disobedient people

2KI 6:24–6:29; ; LAM 2:20, LAM 4:10; ; EZK 5:10

title: 7328: ceremonies
content:
Formal outward practices and rituals symbolising or marking events of importance or spiritual significance. Although they had their place in biblical cultural life, they may become empty and hypocritical, losing their deep spiritual meaning. Ceremonies thus become symbolic of empty legalism which should be rejected.

Ceremonies marking important events

Different stages in life

GEN 50:7, GEN 50:10–50:11; ; EXO 13:2; ; JDG 14:10; ; JER 34:5; ; JHN 2:1–2:2, JHN 20:4

Temple worship

NEH 13:9 See also 1KI 9:25; 1CH 16:40; 2CH 8:13, 2CH 29:15PSA 5:7, PSA 118:19–118:20, PSA 138:2 ACT 3:1

Appointing leaders

ACT 13:2–13:3 See also LEV 8:30; 1SA 16:13; 1KI 1:39; ACT 6:5–6:6, ACT 9:17; 1TI 4:14

Historical events

RUT 4:7–4:8 See also GEN 35:14; EXO 12:14, EXO 13:9; JOS 4:4–4:9; 1SA 7:12; LUK 22:19 parallel 1CO 11:24–11:25

Ceremonies as spiritual symbols of God's presence

MAT 18:20 There is a sense in which any gathering of believers is ceremonially symbolic of God's presence. See also EXO 30:25–30:29; NUM 4:4, NUM 10:35–10:36 The ark of the covenant was a symbol of God's presence among his people. It, and its location, the tabernacle and later, the Most Holy Place, were therefore centres of the most elaborate ceremonies; 1KI 8:3–8:6 parallel 2CH 5:4–5:7; 1KI 8:29 parallel 2CH 6:20; 1CH 6:49; HEB 9:1–9:7

Initiation ceremonies

Circumcision

LEV 12:3 See also GEN 17:10; JOS 5:2; LUK 2:21–2:24

Baptism

MAT 28:19 See also ACT 2:38, ACT 16:33–16:34; 1PE 3:21

Ceremonies relating to ritual cleansing

EXO 30:18–30:20 See also LEV 14:14–14:18, LEV 16:26, LEV 22:6; NUM 19:7; HEB 9:10 Many aspects of ritual activity were added to the biblical ones in Jewish laws and regulations.

Ceremonies relating to atonement

LEV 17:11 See also LEV 4:20, LEV 8:30–8:35, LEV 16:10; HEB 5:1

Ceremonies conveying spiritual realities

Passover: a ceremony symbolising salvation history

LUK 22:15–22:16 See also 2KI 23:21–23:22; EZR 6:21–6:22; MAT 26:17 parallels MRK 14:12, LUK 22:7–22:9

Baptism: a ceremony symbolising new birth

JHN 3:5 See also ACT 10:48, ACT 22:16; GAL 3:27; COL 2:12

Marriage: a ceremony symbolising faithful partnership

MAT 19:6 parallel MRK 10:9; ; EPH 5:25–5:28; ; HEB 13:4; ; REV 19:7

Ceremonies which lapsed into hypocrisy

Washing ceremonies without accompanying inner cleanliness

MAT 23:25 See also MAT 23:27–23:28; MRK 7:4

Sacrificial ceremonies without inner repentance

AMO 5:21–5:22 See also 1SA 15:22; PSA 51:16–51:17; ISA 1:11–1:17; HOS 8:11–8:13; MAL 1:8

Initiation ceremonies without real commitment

1CO 7:19 See also ACT 15:1, ACT 15:8–15:10; ROM 2:28–2:29; GAL 5:6

Jesus Christ brings freedom from meaningless ceremonies

GAL 5:1 See also GAL 4:8–4:10; EPH 2:14–2:18; COL 2:13–2:23; HEB 7:18–7:19

title: 7330: chief priests
content:
A group of individuals in charge of temple worship in Jerusalem, and regarded as leading representatives of the Jewish people, who came into conflict with Jesus Christ and plotted his death.

OT references to the chief priest

2CH 19:11 See also 2KI 25:18 parallel JER 52:24 Seraiah2CH 24:6, 2CH 24:11 2CH 31:10 Azariah; EZR 7:5 Ezra was descended from Aaron the chief priest

The chief priests were in charge of temple worship in Jerusalem

MAT 2:4 They were expected to pronounce on matters of Jewish law and interpretation of Scripture; ; MAT 21:15 Temple discipline was their responsibility.

Jesus Christ predicted his suffering at the hands of the chief priests and elders

MAT 16:21 parallels MRK 8:31, LUK 9:22 See also MAT 20:18 parallel MRK 10:33

The response of the chief priests to Jesus Christ

They were puzzled by Jesus Christ's teaching

MAT 21:23 parallels MRK 11:27–11:28, LUK 20:1–20:2 See also MAT 21:45–21:46 parallel LUK 20:19; JHN 7:32, JHN 7:45, JHN 11:47 As official representatives of the Jewish people, they feared the political repercussions of Jesus Christ's popularity.

They and the elders plotted Jesus Christ's death

MAT 26:3–26:5 parallel MRK 14:1 See also MAT 26:59 parallel MRK 14:55; MAT 27:1

They collaborated with Judas

MAT 26:14 parallels MRK 14:10, LUK 22:4; ; MAT 26:47 parallels MRK 14:43, LUK 22:52, JHN 18:3; ; MAT 27:3–27:6

The role of the chief priests in the trial of Jesus Christ

They made accusations against Jesus Christ

MAT 27:12 parallel MRK 15:3; ; LUK 22:66, LUK 23:10

They persuaded the crowd against Jesus Christ

MAT 27:20; ; LUK 23:4, LUK 23:13; ; JHN 19:6

They mocked Jesus Christ

MAT 27:41 parallel MRK 15:31

They handed Jesus Christ over to Pilate

MRK 15:10; ; LUK 24:20; ; JHN 18:35

They persuaded Pilate to have the tomb guarded

MAT 27:62–27:66

They bribed the soldiers

MAT 28:11–28:15

They rejected Jesus Christ as king

JHN 19:15, JHN 19:21

The chief priests' attempt to prevent the preaching of the gospel

By silencing the apostles

ACT 4:23, ACT 5:24

By authorising Paul to arrest believers

ACT 9:14, ACT 9:21, ACT 26:10, ACT 26:12

The involvement of the chief priests in the arrest and trial of Paul

ACT 22:30, ACT 23:14, ACT 25:2, ACT 25:15

title: 7332: child sacrifice
content:
Child sacrifice is unconditionally condemned in Scripture. The few cases in which it is known to have taken place are probably due to the influence of Israel's pagan neighbours.

Laws forbidding child sacrifice

LEV 18:21 Molech was the god of the Ammonites. See also EXO 22:29 to be interpreted with reference to Ex 13:12–16 and Ex 34:19,22; DEU 18:10

Instances of child sacrifice

2KI 3:27, 2KI 16:3 parallel 2CH 28:3; ; 2KI 17:17, 2KI 17:31, 2KI 21:6 parallel 2CH 33:6; ; 2KI 23:10 Josiah's reforms involved desecrating Topheth, where child sacrifices were offered; ; ISA 57:5; ; JER 7:31

Arguments against child sacrifice

Child sacrifice is the idolatrous practice of the nations

DEU 12:31 See also LEV 20:2–20:5; PSA 106:35–106:38; JER 32:35; HOS 13:2

It defiles God's people

EZK 20:26 See also EZK 20:31

It defiles God's sanctuary

EZK 23:37, EZK 23:39

It incurs God's anger

JER 19:4–19:5; ; EZK 16:20–16:21, EZK 16:36–16:37; ; HOS 13:2

God's unique command to Abraham to sacrifice Isaac

GEN 22:1–22:2 This was a test of obedience for Abraham. The following verses dispel any idea that God desires human sacrifice; ; HEB 11:17; JHN 3:16; ; ROM 8:32

title: 7334: circumcision
content: The practice of cutting away the foreskin of male children, usually within a short time of birth. In the OT, the practice is seen as a sign of membership of the people of God. The NT makes it clear that Christians are under no obligation to be circumcised.

title: 7335: circumcision, physical
content:
In the OT, circumcision is seen as an outward sign of membership of Israel, the people of God.

The significance of circumcision in God's covenant with Abraham

GEN 17:10–17:14 See also ACT 7:8

Circumcision is also for Abraham's descendants

GEN 17:7

Circumcision as a sign of national identity

Dinah's brothers and the Shechemites: GEN 34:8–34:9, GEN 34:14–34:17; EXO 4:24–4:26; ; JOS 5:4–5:8

Circumcision is integrated into the Mosaic law

LEV 12:3; ; JHN 7:22 It was later sometimes forgotten that circumcision predated the Mosaic law.

Circumcision as an important Passover restriction

EXO 12:44, EXO 12:48–12:49; ; JDG 14:3 Some of Israel's neighbours practised circumcision, but not the Philistines.

Circumcision was not necessarily a sign of consecration to God

JER 9:25–9:26

NT accounts of circumcision practised in obedience to the law

LUK 1:59 John; ; LUK 2:21 Jesus Christ; ; JHN 7:23 Circumcision was permitted on the Sabbath; ; PHP 3:5 Paul refers to his own circumcision; ; ACT 16:3 Timothy

The subject of circumcision debated by the first Christians

ACT 10:45, ACT 11:2, ACT 15:5; ; GAL 2:3 That Titus, a Greek, was not required to be circumcised illustrates the change in the attitude of the church concerning the relevance of the law to Gentile believers; ; EPH 2:11; ; COL 3:11; ; TIT 1:10

title: 7336: circumcision, spiritual
content:
On its own, the physical sign of circumcision is no guarantee of finding favour in the sight of God. It was intended to be the outward sign of inward consecration and should be accompanied by repentance, faith and obedience. Paul stresses that circumcision confers no special privileges upon individuals; it is faith in God, rather than any outward sign, that ensures that believers stand in a right relationship to God.

Physical circumcision has no value without the obedience of the heart

DEU 30:6 See also DEU 10:14–10:16; JER 4:4ROM 2:28–2:29 See also ROM 2:25–2:27; 1CO 7:19

Circumcision and faith

GAL 5:6 See also ROM 3:30

Abraham's righteousness depended on his faith, not on the covenant of circumcision

GAL 3:6; ; GEN 15:6; ; ROM 4:9–4:12

Paul values the law as part of God's plan

ROM 3:1–3:2

The sharp debate about circumcision for Christians

The Council of Jerusalem

ACT 15:1–15:19

Jewish Christians in Jerusalem criticise Peter for accepting hospitality from Cornelius and his (uncircumcised) friends

ACT 11:1–11:3

God's acceptance of uncircumcised Gentiles is clear from the outpouring of the Holy Spirit upon them

ACT 10:44–10:46 See also ACT 10:34

Peter's misgivings about Gentile believers and the law

GAL 2:11–2:16 the dispute between Paul and Peter

Imposing circumcision upon Gentile Christians denies the freedom of the gospel

GAL 5:1–5:6 To insist on circumcision is to nullify the work of Jesus Christ on the cross. See also GAL 2:3–2:5, GAL 3:14

``Judaisers'' should be resisted

GAL 5:7–5:12, GAL 6:12–6:13, GAL 6:15; ; PHP 3:2–3:3; ; TIT 1:10–1:11

To be ``in Christ'' is to be spiritually circumcised

COL 2:11–2:12 See also COL 3:11

Paul's confidence in Jesus Christ

PHP 3:4–3:9

The relationship between baptism and circumcision in the NT

COL 2:11–2:13 Both circumcision and baptism may each be described as a “seal”. Baptism signifies entry into the new covenant in the same way that circumcision marked initiation into the old; ; ROM 4:11 Abraham the father of all believers; ; 2CO 1:21–1:22; ; EPH 1:13

title: 7338: cities of refuge
content: Cities set apart at the time of the conquest of Canaan as places of refuge for those who had killed people by accident.

title: 7340: clean and unclean
content:
The distinction between things which were ritually acceptable and unacceptable to God. Animals were classified as either clean or unclean, denoting their suitability or unsuitability for sacrifice and food. The motives and intentions of the heart were also sometimes judged to be clean or unclean, as uncleanness was often linked with sin.

The OT distinction between clean and unclean

The command to distinguish between clean and unclean

LEV 15:31 See also LEV 10:8–10:11, LEV 20:22–20:26; EZK 22:26, EZK 44:23

The distinction between clean and unclean animals

GEN 7:2 See also GEN 7:8–7:9, GEN 8:20; LEV 11:1–11:47 parallel DEU 14:3–14:20; LEV 27:11–27:13

The NT attitude to clean and unclean animals

MRK 7:14–7:23 parallel MAT 15:10–15:20; ; ACT 10:11–10:15, ACT 11:5–11:9

Places and inanimate objects could be clean or unclean

LEV 10:14 See also LEV 4:12, LEV 6:11, LEV 11:32–11:38, LEV 14:33–14:57; 2CH 13:11

Causes of uncleanness

Physical contact with anything unclean

LEV 5:2–5:3 See also LEV 7:19; HOS 9:3–9:4; HAG 2:10–2:14

Contact with corpses

NUM 19:11 See also LEV 21:1–21:4, LEV 21:10–21:12; NUM 6:5–6:8

Infectious skin diseases

LEV 13:1–13:46 See also LEV 14:1–14:32; MAT 8:2–8:4 parallels MRK 1:40–1:44, LUK 5:12–5:14

Certain bodily functions

LEV 12:2 See also LEV 12:5, LEV 15:1–15:33

Consequences of uncleanness

Exclusion from worship

LEV 22:3–22:5 See also LEV 12:4; NUM 9:6–9:12; 1SA 20:24–20:26; 2CH 23:19, 2CH 30:17

Isolation from God's people

NUM 5:2 See also LEV 7:20–7:21; DEU 23:10–23:11

Cleansing from uncleanness

LEV 16:29–16:30 See also LEV 12:6–12:8, LEV 16:15–16:16, LEV 22:4–22:7; NUM 6:9–6:12, NUM 19:1–19:22, NUM 31:21–31:24; HEB 9:13

Inner, spiritual cleanness and uncleanness

JOB 33:9; PSA 51:7 See also GEN 20:5; PSA 24:3–24:4; PRO 20:9; ISA 1:15–1:16, ISA 64:6; LAM 1:8; EZK 36:25; MAT 15:10–15:20 parallel MRK 7:14–7:23; MAT 23:25–23:28; ACT 10:28

title: 7342: cleanliness
content:
Careful, fastidious attention to personal hygiene, particularly important in the climate of the Near East. In Scripture, sin is associated with lack of cleanliness, whereas redemption and baptism are linked with washing.

Cleanliness through bathing

As part of general personal hygiene

RUT 3:1–3:3 See also JHN 13:10

For cleansing from uncleanness

LEV 17:15–17:16 See also LEV 14:8–14:9, LEV 15:13; 2KI 5:9–5:14

Cleanliness through washing feet

A customary practice after a journey

GEN 19:1–19:2 See also GEN 18:1–18:5, GEN 24:32, GEN 43:24; 1SA 25:40–25:42; 2SA 11:6–11:8; LUK 7:36–7:47

As a sign of humility and love

JHN 13:2–13:17 See also 1TI 5:9–5:10

Cleanliness through washing clothes

As part of general personal hygiene

ISA 7:3 See also 2KI 18:17 parallel ISA 36:2

For cleansing from uncleanness

LEV 11:40 See also EXO 19:10–19:11, EXO 19:14–19:15; LEV 13:6, LEV 16:26; NUM 8:7, NUM 19:21

Cleanliness through washing faces and hands

MAT 6:16–6:18 See also EXO 30:18–30:21, EXO 40:30–40:31; MAT 15:2

Cleanliness through washing infants at birth

EZK 16:4–16:5

Cleansing agents

JER 2:22 See also JOB 9:30–9:31; MAL 3:2

The Pharisees' concern with outer cleanliness to the exclusion of inner purity

MRK 7:1–7:23 parallel MAT 15:1–15:20 See also LUK 11:37–11:41

Cleanliness used metaphorically

Lack of cleanliness associated with sin

ZEC 3:1–3:7 See also ISA 4:4; MAT 12:43–12:45 parallel LUK 11:24–11:26

Cleanliness associated with righteousness and purity

REV 19:14 See also JER 4:14; EPH 5:25–5:27; TIT 3:4–3:7; JAS 4:8; REV 15:6, REV 19:8

Baptism compared to washing away sins

ACT 22:16 See also 1PE 3:21–3:22

title: 7344: curtain
content:
Mentioned chiefly in relation to the construction of the tabernacle. The curtain which separated off the Most Holy Place symbolised the separation between God and humanity. The tearing of the curtain when Jesus Christ was crucified symbolised the end of this separation and gave access into God's presence for believers.

Curtains for the construction of the tabernacle

EXO 26:1–26:6 See also EXO 36:8–36:13EXO 26:7–26:13 See also EXO 36:14–36:18EXO 26:36–26:37 See also EXO 36:37–36:38EXO 27:9–27:18 See also EXO 38:9–38:19, EXO 35:10–35:17 parallel EXO 39:33–39:40; NUM 3:25–3:26

The curtain in the Most Holy Place

The Most Holy Place shielded with a curtain

HEB 9:2–9:4 See also EXO 26:31–26:33, EXO 40:2–40:3, EXO 40:21; NUM 4:5; 2CH 3:14 As in the tabernacle, the Most Holy Place in Solomon's temple was shut off with a curtain.

The curtain symbolises separation from God

LEV 16:2 See also NUM 18:7; HEB 9:6–9:9

The curtain indicates the hiddenness of God

PSA 18:11 parallel 2SA 22:12 See also EXO 20:21; DEU 4:11; JOB 22:14; PSA 97:2; 1TI 6:16

The torn curtain symbolises access to God

MAT 27:51 parallels MRK 15:38, LUK 23:45HEB 10:19–10:20 See also HEB 6:19–6:20

Other examples of tent curtains

SNG 1:5; ; ISA 54:2

title: 7346: death penalty
content:
The lawful taking of human life by the civil or military authorities. The death penalty was inflicted by all nations in the ancient world. In Israel, the main method was stoning, amongst other nations, other forms such as hanging, beheading and crucifixion were used.

The ultimate authority for the death penalty lies with God alone

Examples of God taking human life

EXO 12:29 See also NUM 16:35; 1SA 6:19, 1SA 25:38; 2SA 6:7 parallel 1CH 13:10; ACT 12:23

God may delegate his authority to human beings

GEN 9:6; ; JOS 7:15, JOS 7:25; ; 1SA 15:3, 1SA 15:32–15:33

The role of the civil authority

ACT 25:11; ; ROM 13:1, ROM 13:3–13:4

Causes of the death penalty

Sacrilege

EXO 19:12–19:13

Serious abuse of one's parents

EXO 21:15, EXO 21:17; ; DEU 21:18–21:21; ; MAT 15:4

Adultery

LEV 20:10; ; DEU 22:22; ; JHN 8:5

Blasphemy

LEV 24:16; ; JHN 19:7

Idolatry

LEV 20:2

Immorality

LEV 20:11–20:16; ; DEU 22:21–22:25

Kidnapping

EXO 21:16

Murder

GEN 9:6; ; EXO 21:12

Rebellion

DEU 17:12

Sabbath-breaking

EXO 31:14–31:15; ; NUM 15:32–15:36

Witchcraft

LEV 20:27; ; 1SA 28:9

Rape of a betrothed virgin

DEU 22:23–22:29

Bestiality

EXO 22:19

Child sacrifice

LEV 20:2–20:5

Means of executing the death penalty

Beheading

GEN 40:19; ; MRK 6:27 parallel MAT 14:9–14:10

Burning

DAN 3:6

Crucifixion

MAT 27:26 parallels MRK 15:15, JHN 19:16; ; MAT 27:38 parallels MRK 15:27, LUK 23:33

Hanging

GEN 40:22; ; DEU 21:22–21:23; ; ACT 5:30; ; GAL 3:13

Impalement

EZR 6:11; ; EST 2:23, EST 5:14, EST 7:9–7:10, EST 9:12–9:13

Stoning

LEV 24:23; ; DEU 21:18–21:21; LEV 20:2, LEV 24:14; ; NUM 15:35; LEV 24:23; ; NUM 15:36; ; JOS 7:25; 1KI 21:11–21:13; ; 2CH 24:21; ; ACT 7:54–7:59; JHN 8:58–8:59, JHN 10:31–10:33; JHN 8:3–8:11 Jesus Christ refuses to carry out a stoning.

The sword

EXO 32:27; ; 1SA 15:33, 1SA 22:18–22:19; ; 1KI 2:29, 1KI 2:46; ; 2CH 23:14–23:15; ; DAN 2:5; ; ACT 12:2; ; HEB 11:37

Wild beasts

DAN 6:16

The commutation of the death penalty

GEN 4:15; ; EXO 21:13, EXO 21:29–21:30; ; NUM 35:6; ; DEU 4:42, DEU 19:1–19:10

title: 7348: defilement
content:
The result of making oneself or someone else unclean in God's sight. Outward, physical defilement comes from contact with someone or something unclean, whereas sin produces inner, spiritual defilement.

Defilement through contact with anything unclean

LEV 20:25 See also LEV 7:19–7:21, LEV 11:41–11:45, LEV 21:1–21:4, LEV 22:4–22:5; NUM 5:1–5:3, NUM 6:9, NUM 6:12; EZK 4:9–4:15, EZK 44:25; DAN 1:8; HAG 2:10–2:14; MAL 1:7

Defilement through bodily emissions

LEV 12:2–12:7, LEV 15:1–15:17

Defilement by sin

EZK 14:11 See also LEV 19:31; PSA 106:34–106:39; JER 2:23; LAM 4:14; EZK 20:1–20:44, EZK 22:1–22:4, EZK 23:28–23:30, EZK 37:23

Defilement as a result of illicit sexual relations

LEV 18:20 See also GEN 34:1–34:5 rape; GEN 34:27, GEN 49:3–49:4; LEV 18:23–18:30 bestialityLEV 21:7, LEV 21:9, LEV 21:13–21:15 NUM 5:19–5:22, NUM 5:27–5:30; DEU 24:1–24:4; 1CH 5:1; EZK 18:6, EZK 22:11 incest; EZK 23:7, EZK 23:17, EZK 33:26 adultery; REV 14:4

Defilement of God's house

LEV 15:31 NUM 19:13, NUM 19:20; 2CH 29:4–29:5, 2CH 36:14; ; PSA 74:4–74:7, PSA 79:1; JER 7:30, JER 32:32–32:35; EZK 5:11, EZK 9:7, EZK 23:38; ; ACT 21:27–21:28 Paul was falsely accused of bringing Gentiles into the temple area.

Defilement of God's name

EZK 43:7–43:8

Defilement of altars and offerings

EXO 20:25 See also NUM 18:30–18:32; 2KI 23:16, 2KI 23:19

Defilement of the land

NUM 35:34 See also JOS 22:19; ISA 24:5; JER 2:7, JER 3:9, JER 16:18; EZK 36:17–36:18

title: 7350: drink offering
content:
A sacrificial offering of wine poured out at the foot of the altar, to accompany a burnt, fellowship or grain offering.

The drink offering was an ancient custom

GEN 35:14

Drink offerings were proportionate to the size of animal sacrificed

See also NUM 15:5, NUM 15:7, NUM 15:10

Drink offerings were required on feasts and sacred days

As part of the daily offerings: EXO 29:41; ; NUM 28:7–28:8; LEV 23:13 Firstfruits; LEV 23:18; ; NUM 28:31; LEV 23:37; ; NUM 29:30–29:31; NUM 28:24 Passover; ; NUM 28:9–28:10 Sabbath offerings; ; NUM 28:14 monthly offerings; ; NUM 29:6 Feast of Trumpets; ; NUM 29:11 Day of Atonement

Occasions for making a drink offering

To accompany sacrifices for unintentional sins

NUM 15:24

To mark special occasions

NUM 6:17 after taking a Nazirite vow; ; 1CH 29:21 at the accession of a king; ; 2CH 29:35 during a religious reformation; ; EZK 45:17 in Ezekiel's vision of restored worship

God's displeasure when drink offerings were poured out to idols

JER 7:18 See also ISA 57:6; JER 19:13, JER 32:29, JER 44:17–44:19, JER 44:25; EZK 20:28

Drink offerings as a symbol

Of restoration after a locust plague

JOL 2:13–2:14

Of devotion and sacrifice

PHP 2:17 See also 2SA 23:15–23:17; 2TI 4:6–4:8

title: 7352: ephod
content:
A garment worn by priests and high priests

EXO 28:4 See also EXO 28:6–28:13, EXO 39:2–39:7; LEV 8:7; JDG 17:5, JDG 18:17–18:20; 1SA 2:28, 1SA 22:18, 1SA 23:9; HOS 3:4

Worn by people other than priests

2SA 6:14 parallel 1CH 15:27 See also JDG 8:27; 1SA 2:18

title: 7354: feasts and festivals
content: The OT makes reference to a number of feasts and festivals, which generally commemorate an event in Israel's history (e.g., the Feast of Passover) or some season of the year (e.g., the Feast of Weeks). In the NT, some of these festivals are given new meanings, such as Pentecost, which was originally a harvest festival, but which came to celebrate the coming of the Holy Spirit.

title: 7355: feasts and festivals, nature of
content:
Israel's feasts and festivals were to be times of religious and community celebration. Ordained by God, they were to be the outward expression of a right spirital attitude.

Feasts and festivals were appointed by God

EXO 5:1 See also EXO 10:9; HOS 9:5

Israel's three annual festivals

The three pilgrim festivals

EXO 23:14–23:19; 2CH 8:12–8:13 EXO 12:14; ; NUM 28:17; LEV 23:15–23:21; ; NUM 28:26; LEV 23:39–23:41; ; NUM 29:12; ; JDG 21:19; ; 1KI 8:65 parallel 2CH 7:8

They were a joyful response to God's blessings

ZEC 8:19 Contrasting with fasts. See also NAM 1:15

They were celebrated with sacrifices and offerings

NUM 29:39; ; EZK 46:11

Israel's other festivals

New Moon festivals: NUM 10:10; ; 1SA 20:18; ; 1CH 23:31; ; HOS 2:11; EXO 32:5; ; 1KI 12:32–12:33

Festivals should be the outward expression of a right spiritual attitude

ISA 1:14, ISA 29:1; ; HOS 5:7; ; MAL 2:3; ; 1CO 5:8

OT festivals find their fulfilment in Jesus Christ

COL 2:16–2:17

title: 7356: Feast of Dedication
content:
This feast began on 25th Kislev (November/December) and lasted seven days. It celebrated the cleansing of the temple and altar by Judas Maccabeus after they had been defiled by Antiochus Epiphanes.

Jesus Christ was challenged to declare his messiahship at the Feast of Dedication

JHN 10:22–10:24

title: 7357: Feast of Firstfruits
content:
The date of the offering of firstfruits, a celebration of God's gift of the harvest, is not clear; it was probably held on 16th Abib.

Offerings made at the celebration of the firstfruits

A burnt offering and a grain offering

LEV 23:12–23:13

Other offerings

LEV 23:19–23:20

title: 7358: Feast of Tabernacles
content:
This feast began on 15th Tishri (September/October) and lasted seven days. It celebrated the first gathering of the fruit and grain harvests and also God's provision for his people during their wilderness journey from Egypt to the promised land.

Instructions for celebrating the Feast of Tabernacles

EXO 23:16 See also EXO 23:14, EXO 34:22–34:23; LEV 23:33–23:34, LEV 23:39; NUM 29:12; DEU 16:13, DEU 16:14, DEU 16:16

People lived in booths for the duration of the Feast of Tabernacles

LEV 23:40, LEV 23:42–23:43

The offerings to be made on each day of the Feast of Tabernacles

LEV 23:37; ; NUM 29:13–29:38

The Feast of Tabernacles was celebrated throughout Israel's history

2CH 8:12–8:13 under Solomon; EZR 3:4; ; NEH 8:17–8:18; JHN 7:2–7:3, JHN 7:37–7:38

title: 7359: Feast of Trumpets
content:
This feast, which is not specifically mentioned as such in the OT, took place at the observance of the new moon in the month of Tishri (September/October). It signalled the end of the agricultural year.

It was to be a day of rest, commemorated with the sounding of trumpets

LEV 23:24–23:25; ; NUM 29:1

Offerings were made to God

LEV 23:25; ; NUM 29:2–29:5

Celebrated after the exile

NEH 8:2–8:6

title: 7360: Feast of Unleavened Bread
content:
The feast was kept from 15th-21st Abib (March/April), to commemorate the haste with which the Israelites left Egypt. It was always celebrated in the calendar of feasts with the Feast of Passover.

Celebrating the Feast of Unleavened Bread

EXO 12:15–12:20 See also EXO 12:39, EXO 23:15; LEV 23:6; NUM 28:17; DEU 16:3

All use of yeast was forbidden during the Feast of Unleavened Bread

All leaven was to be removed from the home before the feast began

EXO 12:19, EXO 13:7; ; DEU 16:4

No yeast was to be used in the preparation of food

EXO 12:18–12:20, EXO 13:6–13:7, EXO 23:15

Sacrifices made at the Feast of Unleavened Bread

LEV 23:8; ; NUM 28:19–28:24

The Feast of Unleavened Bread in NT times

MRK 14:1, MRK 14:12; ; ACT 12:3; ; 1CO 5:6–5:8

title: 7361: Feast of Weeks
content:
This feast, which took place seven weeks after Passover in Sivan (May/June), was also known as the Feast of Pentecost. It expressed joy and thankfulness to God for the harvest.

The Feast of Weeks was one of three compulsory feasts

EXO 23:16, EXO 34:22

Offerings made at the Feast of Weeks

The firstfruits of the wheat harvest were offered to God

NUM 28:26; ; DEU 16:10 The offerings brought were to be in proportion to the harvest.

Two loaves of bread were also offered

LEV 23:17

Burnt offerings and sin offerings were made

LEV 23:18–23:20 parallel NUM 28:27–28:30

The Feast of Weeks (Pentecost) in the NT

ACT 2:1–2:4 The term “Pentecost” refers to the 50 days that separate this feast from the Passover Sabbath.

title: 7364: fellowship offering
content:
Sacrificial meals shared by offerer, people and priests. The name of these offerings is related to the Hebrew “shalom”, meaning “wholeness” or “peace”, and they are therefore also known as “peace offerings”.

Fellowship offerings were required by God

See also EXO 20:24

Their purpose was thanksgiving, vow fulfilment or freewill offering

LEV 7:11–7:18

Regulations for making a fellowship offering

The animal

LEV 3:1, LEV 3:6, LEV 3:12

Its slaughter

LEV 3:2, LEV 3:8, LEV 3:13, LEV 17:5–17:6

All requirements must be fulfilled for the offering to be acceptable

LEV 19:5, LEV 22:21; ; JOS 22:29; AMO 5:22, AMO 5:24

The fellowship offering was shared

God received the best

LEV 3:3–3:5, LEV 3:9–3:11, LEV 3:14–3:16

The priests received their share as food

LEV 7:29–7:34

The people ate the rest

LEV 10:14

Fellowship offerings affirmed the covenant relationship

The Sinai covenant with Israel: EXO 20:24, EXO 24:4–24:6; ; JOS 8:31–8:32; 1SA 10:8, 1SA 11:15; ; 1KI 9:25

The celebration of fellowship offerings

At seasonal festivals

LEV 23:19 the Feast of Weeks; ; NUM 29:39 the Feast of Tabernacles

On other religious and national occasions

At the completion of the time of separation of a Nazirite: NUM 6:14, NUM 6:17–6:18; NUM 10:10 at New Moons and other festivals; NUM 7:17, NUM 7:23, NUM 7:29; NUM 15:8; ; DEU 27:7; 2SA 6:17–6:18 on bringing the ark to Jerusalem; ; 2SA 24:25 on locating the site for the temple; 1KI 8:63–8:64 parallel 2CH 7:7 at the dedication of the temple; 2CH 29:35, 2CH 30:22, 2CH 31:2, 2CH 33:16; PRO 7:14; EZK 45:15, EZK 45:17, EZK 46:2, EZK 46:12

Fellowship offerings in idolatrous worship

EXO 32:6

title: 7366: freewill offering
content:
An offering that in some ways resembled the thank-offering or peace offering. It was completely voluntary, prompted by an occasion of celebration or spiritual significance. The animal offered was eaten at a festive banquet.

Instructions for presenting a freewill offering

The animal to be offered

LEV 22:17–22:22, LEV 22:23 Some slightly deformed animals were perhaps acceptable because the freewill offering was voluntary.

Eating the offering

LEV 7:16

Freewill offerings were associated with joy and celebration

PSA 54:6–54:7 See also DEU 16:9–16:12 during the Feast of Weeks

Occasions for making freewill offerings

In maintaining public worship and at times of religious renewal

EXO 35:20–35:29 Building the temple. See also 2CH 31:14 Hezekiah's reformationEZR 1:4–1:6, EZR 2:68, EZR 7:16, EZR 8:28; NEH 7:70–7:72 EZK 46:11–46:12 Ezekiel's visionary ideal temple

As an additonal offering at certain festivals

NUM 29:39; DEU 12:5–12:6, DEU 12:17–12:18

Prophetic criticism of bragging over freewill offerings

AMO 4:5 A sarcastic attack on the offering of freewill gifts as a way to gain prestige.

title: 7368: grain offering
content:
An offering that was usually offered as an accompaniment to animal sacrifices, but in special circumstances was presented alone. It could be cooked in various ways or presented uncooked. The offering could be made for a variety of purposes, depending on the sacrifice it accompanied.

The Hebrew word for grain offering'' can also mean simplygift''

See also GEN 4:4, GEN 32:13; 1SA 2:17; 1KI 4:21; MRK 7:11

The ingredients of the grain offering

Flour, oil and incense

LEV 2:1–2:2

Salt

See also LEV 2:13 Salt, which prevented corruption, was offered with all the offerings.

No yeast or honey

LEV 2:11

Roasted ears of corn

LEV 2:14–2:16

The preparation of the grain offering

LEV 2:4 oven-baked like bread; ; LEV 2:5–2:6 on a griddle or in a pan

The presentation of the grain offering

By fire

LEV 2:2 See also LEV 2:3 It might be eaten, but only by the priests and in the sanctuary.

Accompanying vow fulfilment or freewill offerings

NUM 15:1–15:10

With an animal sacrifice

NUM 15:6 See also NUM 15:8–15:9 the larger the animal, the larger the grain offering

With the two daily offerings

EXO 29:38–29:41

Circumstances under which the grain offering was presented alone

LEV 6:19–6:23 when a priest was consecrated; NUM 5:15, NUM 5:25

The character of the grain offering

It is most holy

LEV 6:17

It is an aroma pleasing to the LORD

See also LEV 2:2, LEV 2:9

title: 7370: guilt offering
content:
An offering that resembled the sin offering, but was offered especially in cases where restitution could be made for an unintentional sin.

Circumstances for making the guilt offering

Unintentional sin against God

LEV 5:17 See also LEV 5:15

Sin against a neighbour where restitution can be made

LEV 5:16 Where restitution is possible it is a requirement; ; LEV 6:1–6:5 A surcharge of twenty per cent was to be added; ; NUM 5:5–5:8 Restitution must be made to God if the wronged person is not accessible.

The nature of the guilt offering

A ram without defect

LEV 5:18

Regulations governing the offering

See also LEV 7:1–7:6; NUM 18:9

Other situations requiring a guilt offering

LEV 14:12–14:14 cleansing from infectious diseases; ; LEV 19:20–19:22 wronging a man by intercourse with a slave girl promised to him; ; NUM 6:12 cleansing a Nazirite after contact with a dead body; ; EZR 10:19 for returning exiles who had intermarried

Guilt offerings in Ezekiel's vision of the temple

EZK 40:38–40:39, EZK 42:13, EZK 44:29, EZK 46:20

The death of the ``suffering servant'' is interpreted as a guilt offering

ISA 53:10

title: 7372: hands, laying on of
content:
An action of God or human beings, often understood to convey healing, blessing or the gift of the Holy Spirit. It also indicates the conferring of authority upon individuals for the purpose of ministry.

Laying on of hands and the communication of gifts

Laying on of hands communicates God's blessing

MRK 10:16 parallel MAT 19:15 See also GEN 48:14–48:20

Laying on of hands conveys God's healing

LUK 4:40 See also MAT 8:3 parallels MRK 1:41, LUK 5:13 the man with leprosy; MAT 9:18 parallel MRK 5:23 Jairus' daughter; MAT 9:29–9:30; MRK 6:5, MRK 7:32–7:35, MRK 16:18; LUK 13:13 the crippled woman; ACT 28:8; JAS 5:14

Laying on of hands conveys the gift of the Holy Spirit

ACT 8:17 See also DEU 34:9; ACT 8:19, ACT 9:17, ACT 19:6

Laying on of hands conveys reassurance

REV 1:17 See also MAT 17:7

Laying on of hands in commissioning for service

NUM 27:18–27:23 See also NUM 8:10; DEU 34:9; ACT 6:6, ACT 13:3; 1TI 4:14, 1TI 5:22; 2TI 1:6

Laying on of hands in the OT sacrificial system

Laying on of hands in sin offerings

LEV 16:21 See also EXO 29:10; LEV 4:4, LEV 4:15, LEV 8:14; 2CH 29:23

Laying on of hands in other offerings

LEV 1:4 See also EXO 29:15, EXO 29:19; LEV 3:1–3:2, LEV 8:18, LEV 8:22

Laying on of hands without reference to God

Laying hands on people with intent to harm

GEN 37:27; ; JOS 2:19; ; 1SA 23:7, 1SA 26:9–26:11, 1SA 26:23; ; ISA 11:14; ; LUK 21:12

Laying on of hands in punishment

LEV 24:14; ; NEH 13:21

The metaphorical laying of God's hand on people

God's hand brings favour

NEH 2:8 See also EZR 7:6, EZR 7:28, EZR 8:18; NEH 2:18

God's hand brings protection

EZR 8:31 See also EZR 7:9

God's hand brings prophetic revelation

EZK 1:3 See also EZK 3:14, EZK 3:22, EZK 8:1, EZK 33:22, EZK 37:1, EZK 40:1

God's hand brings judgment

EXO 7:4 See also 1SA 5:6–5:12; JER 15:6; EZK 39:21; ACT 13:11

title: 7374: high places
content:
Natural heights were sometimes the sites of shrines devoted to the LORD. However, the term usually refers to the places where shrines were set up to foreign gods, especially shrines where the worship of the LORD was in rivalry with Jerusalem.

High places referring to mountainous areas

HAB 3:19 See also DEU 32:13; PSA 18:33 parallel 2SA 22:34

High places express divine lordship

AMO 4:13 See also MIC 1:3

High places referring to shrines to the LORD

High places were the focus of worship before the building of Solomon's temple

1KI 3:2–3:3

High places associated with Samuel

1SA 9:12 See also 1SA 9:13–9:14, 1SA 9:19

Associated with other prophets

1SA 10:5

High places referring to foreign shrines

In Canaan before the conquest

DEU 33:29

Among Israel's neighbours

ISA 15:2 See also ISA 16:12

Israelite use of high places condemned

The building of shrines on high places

1KI 12:31

High places condemned by God

LEV 26:30 See also NUM 33:52; DEU 12:2

High places condemned by the prophets

EZK 20:28–20:29 See also JER 17:3

High places associated with foreign gods

JER 32:35 Baal; ; 1KI 11:7 Chemosh and Molech

Worship at the high places

High places regarded as unsatisfactory places to worship the LORD

2CH 32:12 See also 2KI 18:4 The presence of Asherah poles is evidence of a consort for the LORD, typical of Baal worship, but alien to the Biblical concept of God; 2CH 31:1

Characteristics of worship at the high places

2KI 17:11 burning incense; ; 2KI 23:20 the presence of priests; ; 2CH 28:25 sacrifices; ; EZK 43:7 a site for the tombs of kings

Removal of the high places

LEV 26:30; ; NUM 33:52; ; 2CH 14:3, 2CH 17:6

Commended for the zeal shown

2KI 23:5 See also 2KI 23:8, 2KI 23:15

Used as a test

1KI 15:14, 1KI 22:43; ; 2KI 12:2–12:3, 2KI 14:3–14:4, 2KI 15:3–15:4, 2KI 15:34–15:35; ; 2CH 20:32–20:33

title: 7376: high priest
content: One of the most important people in Israel, responsible for the spiritual welfare of the nation and, in particular, for making atonement for the sins of the people. The NT recognises Jesus Christ as bringing this role to fulfilment, making the perfect sacrifice for the sins of his people, as both high priest and the sacrificial victim.

title: 7377: high priest, in OT
content:
The OT emphasises the spiritual importance of the high priest, especially as the one who makes atonement for the sins of the nation. It also sets out his duties and responsibilities.

The role of the high priest

Making atonement on the Day of Atonement

LEV 16:3 See also LEV 16:1–16:34

Teaching

DEU 33:10 See also EZR 7:12; NEH 8:2

Providing oracles

LEV 8:8 See also NUM 27:21; DEU 33:8

Interceding

EZR 9:5

Administering the sanctuary

NUM 3:38

Anointing kings

2KI 11:12

Supervising censuses

NUM 26:1–26:2

Encouraging soldiers

DEU 20:2

Titles used to refer to the high priest

The priest

EXO 31:10

The anointed priest

LEV 4:3 See also EXO 30:30; LEV 4:5, LEV 4:16, LEV 16:32

The high priest

LEV 21:10 See also HAG 1:12–1:14, HAG 2:2–2:4; ZEC 3:1–3:8, ZEC 6:11

Melchizedek, the king-priest

GEN 14:18; ; PSA 110:4 David's son, as king-priest, is appointed to the order of Melchizedek, a priesthood superior to that of Aaron; ; HEB 7:11

High priesthood in the law

Only certain people might be high priests

NUM 3:10

Consecration of a high priest

LEV 8:30

Dignity of the high priesthood

DEU 17:12 See also LEV 21:10

Dress of the high priest

EXO 28:4 See also LEV 8:7–8:9, LEV 16:4

Other high priests mentioned in the OT

1SA 1:9 Eli; ; 1SA 21:2 Ahimelech; ; 1CH 24:3 Zadok; ; 2KI 16:10 Uriah; ; 2KI 22:4 Hilkiah; ; 2KI 25:18 Seraiah; ; NEH 3:1 Eliashib

title: 7378: high priest, in NT
content:
Under the Roman administration the high priest remained the senior Jewish leader, but his power was limited by Roman supervision.

High priests of the NT

LUK 3:2; ; ACT 23:2 Annas was high priest from A.D. 6 until deposed by the Roman official, Gratus, in A.D. 15. Though replaced by his son Eleazar and, in A.D. 18, by Caiaphas, his son-in-law, Annas retained his authority in Jewish eyes. Strictly, there could only be one high priest at a time. Ananias, the high priest of Ac 23:2, was not related to the family of Annas.

Grandeur of the high priest

JHN 18:10, JHN 18:15 The courtyard was within a palace; ; ACT 6:12, ACT 7:1 The high priest was president of the Sanhedrin.

The high priest's connections with Jesus Christ

Prophesying Jesus Christ's death

JHN 11:49–11:50 Words spoken by Caiaphas in a political sense were seen to be prophetic, pointing forward to the sacrificing role the high priest would play in the death of Jesus Christ. See also JHN 11:51–11:52

Presiding at Jesus Christ's trial

JHN 18:19–18:24 See also MAT 26:57–26:59 parallel MRK 14:53–14:55; MAT 26:62–26:65 parallel MRK 14:61–14:64; LUK 22:54; JHN 18:28 Caiaphas, the necessary go-between, brought Jesus Christ before Pilate.

The high priest's encounters with Peter and John

ACT 4:7 The teaching of Jesus Christ's resurrection was particularly abhorrent to the Sadducees, the priestly party to which Annas and Caiaphas belonged. See also ACT 5:17, ACT 5:21, ACT 5:29

The high priest's encounter with Stephen

ACT 7:1 Stephen was thought to have threatened the temple, the focus of high priestly power. See also ACT 7:57–7:59

Encounters of the high priests with Paul

Commissioning Paul to persecute Christians

ACT 9:1–9:2

Ordering that Paul be struck

ACT 23:3 See also JHN 18:21–18:23

Taking charges against Paul to the Roman governor

ACT 24:1 See also JHN 18:28

NT references to OT high priests

Abiathar

MRK 2:26

Melchizedek

HEB 5:6

Jesus Christ as high priest

HEB 4:14–4:16 See also HEB 8:1–8:2, HEB 9:14

title: 7379: high priest, Jesus Christ as
content: See 2306: Jesus Christ, as high priest

title: 7382: house of God
content:
The place where God was seen to dwell with his people, initially the tabernacle and subsequently the temple at Jerusalem.

Early references to the house of God

GEN 28:16–28:17 “house of God” was first used by Jacob to commemorate the place where he met with God; GEN 28:19 “Bethel” means “house of God”. See also GEN 28:22

The tabernacle as the house of God

The tabernacle was referred to as such before its construction

EXO 23:19 See also EXO 34:26

In the wilderness God said he would dwell with his people in the tabernacle

EXO 25:8–25:9

God's presence came and dwelt in the tabernacle when it was completed

EXO 40:33–40:34 See also EXO 40:35–40:38

The temple as the permanent house of God

David's desire to build the LORD a permanent house

2SA 7:1–7:2

This was not the LORD's will for David

2SA 7:5–7:7, 2SA 7:12–7:13 See also ACT 7:45–7:47

Solomon builds the temple in Jersualem

1KI 5:4–5:5, 1KI 6:1–6:2

God's presence dwelt there but was conditional upon the faithfulness of the people

1KI 8:10–8:13 parallel 2CH 5:11–6:2 See also 1KI 9:6–9:9

Zerubbabel's temple referred to as the house of God

EZR 1:5 EZR 2:68, EZR 3:8–3:9, EZR 3:11, EZR 4:24, EZR 5:2, EZR 5:13, EZR 5:16–5:17; EZR 10:1, EZR 10:6, EZR 10:9; NEH 6:10, NEH 8:16, NEH 10:32, NEH 10:34–10:38, NEH 11:11, NEH 11:22, NEH 12:40; NEH 13:9, NEH 13:11, NEH 13:14

Other names given to the house of God

Sanctuary

EXO 25:8

House of prayer

MAT 21:13 parallels MRK 11:17, LUK 19:46; ; ISA 56:7

God's dwelling-place

ACT 7:45–7:47

The need for due reverence in worship

ECC 5:1

The house of God under the new covenant

In the hearts of God's people

1CO 3:16; GAL 4:6 See also ACT 7:48, ACT 17:24; 1CO 6:19; 2CO 1:22, 2CO 6:16; EPH 3:17

In the church, the body of Christ

HEB 3:6 See also EPH 2:19–2:22; 1PE 2:4–2:5

title: 7384: household gods
content:
Known in the Hebrew as “teraphim”, miniature idols small enough for their owners to keep in their homes or to carry with them when travelling.

Examples of the use of household gods

GEN 31:19 See also GEN 31:34; JDG 17:1–17:6, JDG 18:18, JDG 18:30–18:31; 1SA 19:13–19:16; EZK 21:21 Three methods of divination are described here, shaking the arrows (belomancy), consulting the household gods (necromancy) and the examination of the liver of a sacrificed victim (hepatoscopy).

The use of household gods was contrary to God's law

DEU 7:25–7:26 See also EXO 20:4–20:6 parallel DEU 5:8–5:10

The removal and condemnation of household gods

2KI 23:24 See also GEN 35:2–35:4; ISA 44:9–44:11; EZK 18:5–18:6; ZEC 10:2

title: 7386: incense
content:
An expensive resin from southern Arabia and the fragrant aroma it emits when offered in worship ritual. It is also called frankincense.

Regulations governing the offering of incense

The priest was to keep incense burning on the altar daily

2CH 13:11 See also EXO 30:7–30:9; NUM 4:16; DEU 33:10; 1SA 2:28; 1CH 6:49, 1CH 9:29; LUK 1:9

Only the priests were to burn incense

Korah, Dathan and Abiram: NUM 16:16–16:18, NUM 16:35, NUM 16:40, NUM 16:46–16:47; 2CH 26:16–26:19 Uzziah's pride

Failure to burn incense is disobedience

2CH 29:7–29:8

The blending of the incense

EXO 30:34–30:38 See also EXO 25:6 parallel EXO 35:8; EXO 35:28

Occasions for offering incense

At the setting up of the tabernacle: EXO 39:38, EXO 40:5, EXO 40:26–40:27; LEV 2:1–2:2, LEV 2:15–2:16, LEV 6:15; LEV 5:11 not to accompany a sin offering; ; LEV 16:12 on the Day of Atonement; ; LEV 24:7 to accompany bread and oil; ; NUM 7:86 As part of the offerings at the dedication of the tabernacle, each leader presented a golden incense dish.

The altar of incense

EXO 30:1 See also EXO 30:27, EXO 31:8, EXO 31:11, EXO 35:15, EXO 37:25–37:29; 1CH 28:18

Burning incense to foreign gods

Incense offered to other gods will not bring answers to prayer

JER 11:12, JER 44:23

Israel disobeyed the LORD's commands

JER 7:9–7:10 See also JER 18:15, JER 19:13, JER 32:29, JER 44:5; EZK 16:18–16:19, EZK 20:28; HOS 11:2

The practice continued under successive kings

In the early days of Solomon's reign: 1KI 3:3, 1KI 11:8; 1KI 22:43 Jehoshaphat; ; 2KI 12:3 Joash; ; 2KI 14:4 Amaziah; ; 2KI 15:4 Azariah; ; 2KI 15:35 Jotham; ; 2KI 16:4 parallel 2CH 28:4 Ahaz

It provoked God's anger

JER 44:3 See also 2KI 22:17; ISA 65:3; JER 1:16, JER 11:17, JER 44:5, JER 44:8, JER 44:21, JER 48:35; HOS 2:13

The destruction of pagan incense altars

As part of God's punishment for disobedience

LEV 26:30; ; EZK 6:4, EZK 6:6, EZK 6:13

Their destruction in religious reformations

2KI 18:4 under Hezekiah; 2KI 23:5, 2KI 23:8; ; 2CH 34:4, 2CH 34:7; NEH 13:5, NEH 13:9

Their destruction brought God's blessing and deliverance

ISA 27:9

The significance of incense

A right heart attitude is more important to God

ISA 66:3; ; JER 6:20

It both accompanies and symbolises prayer

PSA 141:2; ; REV 5:8, REV 8:3–8:4

It symbolises worship

2CH 2:4; ; DAN 2:46; ; MAL 1:11; ; MAT 2:11; ; HEB 9:4

It brings pleasure

PRO 27:9; ; SNG 3:6, SNG 4:6; ; EZK 20:41

title: 7388: kinsman-redeemer
content:
The relative who restores or preserves the full community rights of disadvantaged family members. The concept arises from God's covenant relationship with Israel and points to the redemption of humanity in Jesus Christ.

Covenant rules for the kinsman-redeemer

The kinsman-redeemer's obligation to redeem the land

LEV 25:25–25:28; ; JER 32:6–32:9

The kinsman-redeemer's obligation to redeem the enslaved

LEV 25:47–25:55

The kinsman-redeemer's obligation to provide an heir

GEN 38:8–38:10; ; DEU 25:5–25:10; ; MAT 22:23–22:28 parallels MRK 12:18–12:23, LUK 20:27–20:33

The kinsman-redeemer's obligation to avenge death

NUM 35:16–35:21

The kinsman-redeemer's obligation to be a trustee

NUM 5:5–5:8

The kinsman-redeemer in the book of Ruth

RUT 2:20 See also RUT 3:1–4:17

The LORD as redeemer

EXO 6:6–6:7 See also 2SA 7:22–7:24; ISA 43:1–43:7, ISA 54:5–54:8; JER 50:33–50:34

The kinsman-redeemer reflects God's concern for the poor and oppressed

PRO 23:10–23:11 See also PSA 68:5–68:6, PSA 72:2–72:4

God's provision of Jesus Christ as kinsman-redeemer

GAL 4:4–4:7 See also GAL 3:13–3:14; HEB 2:11–2:18

resource.label: 6717: reconciliation, of the world to God

title: 7390: Levites
content:
Assistants to the Aaronic priests, they exercised an auxiliary ministry, caring for the tabernacle. After the ark was brought to Jerusalem by David their role was re-ordered and extended. They were reconsecrated during the reforms of Josiah and Hezekiah and recommissioned by Ezra and Nehemiah when the Israelites returned from captivity.

The Levites and the care of the tabernacle

The Levites are to transport, erect and guard the tabernacle

NUM 1:48–1:53 See also NUM 2:17; DEU 10:8

The Gershonite branch of the Levites are to care for the coverings and curtains of the tabernacle

NUM 3:25–3:26 See also NUM 4:24–4:27

The Kohathite branch of the Levites are to care for the furnishings of the sanctuary

NUM 3:31 See also NUM 4:4–4:20

The Merarite branch of the Levites are to care for the frames of the tabernacle

NUM 3:36–3:37 See also NUM 4:29–4:33

The Levites are to have an auxiliary ministry

NUM 3:5–3:10 See also NUM 8:19, NUM 18:1–18:7

The Levites are set apart for their ministry

NUM 8:20–8:21 See also NUM 8:5–8:15

The Levites' period of service was between the ages of 25 and 50

NUM 8:23–8:26 See also NUM 4:1–4:3 Some suggest that the reason for the discrepancy in ages between Numbers chapters 4 and 8 is that the Levites had to serve an apprenticeship for the first five years.

Occasions after the settlement when the Levites transport the ark

1SA 6:15; ; 2SA 15:24; ; 1KI 8:3–8:5 parallel 2CH 5:4–5:6; ; 1CH 15:1–15:16

David re-orders and extends the role of the Levites

1CH 23:2–23:6 See also 1CH 6:31–6:49, 1CH 16:4–16:6, 1CH 23:24–23:32, 1CH 25:1–26:32

Hezekiah and Josiah rededicate the Levites

2CH 29:3–29:36, 2CH 35:1–35:6

Ezra and Nehemiah recommission the Levites after the return from exile

EZR 6:16–6:18 See also NEH 12:22–12:26

Ezekiel prophesies a restored but restricted role for the Levites in the new temple

EZK 44:10–44:14

title: 7392: lots, casting of
content:
A means of determining the will of God, prior to the giving of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost. The casting of lots was also used by pagans for the same purpose. Such use reflects the belief that nothing occurred by chance.

Casting lots to determine the will of God

PRO 16:33

In the ministry of the high priest

EXO 28:30 The “Urim and Thummim” were sacred lots maintained for the purpose of determining God's will. See also LEV 8:7–8:9, LEV 16:6–16:10; NUM 27:21; DEU 33:8; 1SA 28:6; EZR 2:63 parallel NEH 7:65

To apportion land

NUM 33:54 See also NUM 26:54–26:56; JOS 14:2, JOS 18:10

To select individuals

1SA 14:41–14:42 See also JOS 7:14–7:18; JDG 20:9–20:10; 1SA 10:20–10:21; JON 1:7ACT 1:15–1:26

To assign priestly duties

1CH 24:5 See also 1CH 26:12–26:13; NEH 10:34; LUK 1:8–1:9

To settle disputes

PRO 18:18

Casting lots as a means of divination

EZK 21:21–21:22 See also EST 3:7, EST 9:24–9:27 The word “purim” is the plural of “pur” and means “lots”.

Casting lots as a means of distributing plunder

JOL 3:2–3:3 See also NAM 3:10MAT 27:35 parallels MRK 15:24, LUK 23:34, JHN 19:24 See also PSA 22:18

title: 7394: memorial
content:
An object, institution or custom established or founded as a reminder of an important person or event held to be worth remembering in the life of the community.

Objects as a memorial

Stones

JOS 4:4–4:7 See also EXO 28:9–28:12 parallel EXO 39:6–39:7; EXO 28:29

Money or gold

EXO 30:16 See also NUM 31:54

Incense

LEV 24:7 See also ISA 66:3

A crown

ZEC 6:9–6:15

Offerings as a memorial

LEV 2:1–2:2 See also LEV 5:11–5:13; NUM 10:10

Meals as a memorial

The Lord's Supper

1CO 11:23–11:26 See also MAT 26:26–26:28 parallels MRK 14:22–14:24, LUK 22:17–22:20

The Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread

DEU 16:1–16:8 parallels EXO 12:14–12:20, LEV 23:4–23:8, NUM 28:16–28:25

title: 7396: Most Holy Place
content:
The inner sanctuary of the tabernacle set up at Sinai and of Solomon's temple, where the ark of the covenant was kept. It is a symbol of the unapproachable presence of God.

Terms used to describe the Most Holy Place

EXO 26:33 “the Most Holy Place” (or “Holy of Holies”), in contrast to the “Holy Place” where the altar stood. This superlative suggests increasing closeness to the presence of God. The distinction is not always observed, however. In Leviticus chapter 16 “Holy Place” is used regularly (translated in NIV as “Most Holy Place” in verses 2, 16, 17, 20, 27, and as “sanctuary area” in verse 3, though it is the inner sanctuary that is referred to, as the phrase “behind the curtain” (verse 2) shows); ; 1KI 6:16 the “inner sanctuary” in contrast to the outer; ; HEB 9:7 literally “the second (tabernacle)” in contrast to the first or outer

The structure and design of the Most Holy Place

In the wilderness tabernacle

The only details concern the curtains forming the walls of the two sanctuaries, and in particular the dividing curtain which separates the Most Holy Place from the outer sanctuary: EXO 26:31–26:33, EXO 36:35–36:36, EXO 40:21

In Solomon's temple

1KI 6:16, 1KI 6:19–6:20 parallel 2CH 3:8; ; 1KI 6:23–6:28 parallel 2CH 3:10; ; 1KI 6:31–6:32; ; 2CH 3:14

In the temple in Ezekiel's vision

EZK 41:4, EZK 41:15–41:21, EZK 41:23, EZK 45:3 In Ezekiel's vision a portion of the land was sacred to the LORD and called the Most Holy Place.

The significance and function of the Most Holy Place

To house the ark of the covenant

EXO 26:33 See also EXO 40:3; 1KI 6:19, 1KI 8:6–8:9 parallel 2CH 5:7–5:9

To house the incense altar (in Solomon's temple)

1KI 6:22; HEB 9:3–9:4 Ex 30:6 and 40:5 suggest that the incense altar was housed in front of the curtain, i.e., in the Holy Place, the outer sanctuary.

To be a symbol of the presence of God

PSA 28:2 See also EXO 26:33 The curtain veiling the Most Holy Place represents the unapproachable presence of the Holy God; LEV 16:2

The Most Holy Place on the Day of Atonement

HEB 9:7 See also LEV 16:16–16:17

The NT understanding of the Most Holy Place as the place of God's heavenly presence

Jesus Christ enters the Most Holy Place as Mediator

HEB 9:8 See also HEB 6:19–6:20, HEB 9:11–9:12, HEB 9:24

Entry to the Most Holy Place is open to all believers

HEB 10:19–10:22 See also MAT 27:51 parallel LUK 23:45 By Jesus Christ's death the dividing curtain has been torn in two.

title: 7398: New Moon festival
content:
The festival which marked the consecration to God of each new month in the year.

New Moon festivals were required under the Mosaic law

NUM 28:11

New Moon festivals were times of celebration

They were marked by blowing of trumpets

NUM 10:10 See also PSA 81:3

They were celebrated with offerings

NUM 28:11–28:15; ; 1CH 23:30–23:31; ; 2CH 2:4, 2CH 8:12–8:13, 2CH 31:3 reformed temple worship under Hezekiah; ; NEH 10:32–10:33 New Moon festivals were restored on the return from exile; ; EZK 45:17, EZK 46:1, EZK 46:3, EZK 46:6

They were times when normal work ceased

David was to dine with Saul: 1SA 20:5, 1SA 20:18, 1SA 20:24; 2KI 4:23 the Shunammite woman and her husband; ; AMO 8:5 Some saw them as a hindrance to business.

A wrong inner attitude at New Moon celebrations incurs God's anger

ISA 1:13–1:14; ; HOS 2:11, HOS 5:7

NT reference to the New Moon festival

COL 2:16–2:17

title: 7400: New Year, the
content:
With the exception of one reference (Ex 12:2) the New Year is never specifically dated. The religious calendar apparently began in the spring and the agricultural calendar in the autumn. Later Judaism began its calendar in the autumn.

Evidence that the autumn was regarded as the New Year

The dedication of Solomon's temple

1KI 8:2 Solomon waited 11 months after the completion of the temple (see 1Ki 6:38), before dedicating it, most likely because the great Feast of Tabernacles marked the beginning of the year.

Instructions for celebrating the Year of Jubilee

LEV 25:8–25:9 The Year of Jubilee was to be proclaimed on the tenth day of the seventh month, which was also the Day of Atonement. See also LEV 16:29

Ezekiel's vision of the new temple

EZK 40:1 This vision of the new temple takes place at the beginning of the year. The “tenth of the month” refers presumably to the seventh month.

Jeroboam's institution of an autumnal festival

1KI 12:32 This autumn festival was clearly intended to be equivalent not only to the feast in Judah, but also to other pagan New Year festivals, in spite of the eighth month being distinctive. See also 1KI 12:33

Evidence that the spring was regarded as the New Year

The institution of the Passover

EXO 12:2 This clearly links the spring Feast of Passover to the New Year. See also LEV 23:5; NUM 9:2

Ezekiel's vision of the worship of the new temple

EZK 45:18–45:19 This purification of Ezekiel's (ideal) temple resembles the Day of Atonement but takes place in the spring. See also EZK 45:21

Occasions associating the New Year with the autumn

The sounding of trumpets

NUM 29:1 See also LEV 23:24

The Day of Atonement

LEV 23:27

The Feast of Tabernacles

DEU 16:13 See also LEV 23:34

Occasions associating the New Year with the spring

Passover

LEV 23:5 See also EXO 12:11

The Feast of Unleavened bread

LEV 23:6 See also EXO 12:17

The Feast of Firstfruits

LEV 23:10–23:11 This is also known as the Feast of Weeks (Ex 23:16; Lev 23:9–21).

title: 7402: offerings
content:
Anything offered up to God by human beings, including sacrificial offerings. A distinction can be made between offerings which involve the taking of life and other gifts made to God.

Sacrificial offerings as a sign of gratitude or repentance

GEN 4:4; PSA 107:22

Tithes were a regular form of offering

GEN 28:20–28:22; LEV 27:30 See also GEN 14:20

Part of every harvest was given as a freewill offering to God

As firstfruits: EXO 22:29; ; NUM 18:12; ; DEU 18:4; ; NEH 10:35; LEV 19:23–19:25 Fruit from a tree could not be eaten until the fifth year; ; LEV 23:38 Extra gifts were offered on the Sabbath.

Offerings and the tabernacle

For its construction

EXO 25:1–25:2 parallel EXO 35:5

At its dedication

See also NUM 7:2–7:3

Offerings and Solomon's temple

David's personal gift

1CH 29:2–29:5

Offerings for upkeep and restoration

In the reign of Joash: 2KI 12:4–12:5; ; 2CH 24:8–24:12; 2KI 22:4–22:6 parallel 2CH 34:9–34:11

Offerings and the second temple

EZR 3:5, EZR 7:16, EZR 8:24–8:30

Offerings and Herod's temple

MRK 12:41–12:43 parallel LUK 21:1–21:3 The poor widow was participating in a regular practice; ; LUK 21:5

Some offerings are not acceptable

MAT 5:23–5:24 Offerings are unacceptable unless the worshipper is reconciled to God. See also GEN 4:3–4:5 Cain's offering was not accepted on account of his incorrect attitude.AMO 4:4–4:5; LUK 18:12 MAT 23:23 parallel LUK 11:42 Meticulous tithing, without mercy or justice, is unacceptable.

Offerings in the future kingdom

From the Gentile nations

PSA 76:11; ; ISA 18:7; ; MAL 1:11

From God's people

EZK 20:40, EZK 44:30

Gifts and offerings in the NT

To help the needy

ACT 4:34–4:35, ACT 11:29–11:30; ; 1CO 16:1–16:4 In several of his letters Paul mentions the freewill offering he was organising for poor Christians at Jerusalem; ; HEB 13:16

To support God's servants

PHP 4:10–4:18

Offerings of praise

HEB 13:15

God's people are themselves an offering to God

JER 2:3; ; JAS 1:18; ; REV 14:4

Offerings as ``Corban''

MRK 7:9–7:13 “Corban” is a technical Jewish religious term, meaning “something dedicated, especially on oath”. What Jesus Christ says implies that once the children had donated the amount they would have spent on their ageing parents for religious purposes, they were no longer responsible for their upkeep.

title: 7404: ordinances
content:
Specific acts of worship commanded by God, sometimes including the sacraments.

The ordinance of Passover and Unleavened Bread

EXO 12:14 See also EXO 12:17, EXO 12:24, EXO 12:43, EXO 13:10; NUM 9:12

The ordinance of the Day of Atonement

LEV 16:34 See also LEV 16:29, LEV 16:31, LEV 23:31

The ordinance of the red-heifer ritual

NUM 19:2, NUM 19:10, NUM 19:21

Other occasions of worship as ordinances

LEV 23:41, LEV 24:3; ; NUM 31:21; ; 2CH 2:4; ; PSA 81:4; ; EZK 46:14

Ordinances associated with the priesthood

The Aaronic priests

EXO 29:9 See also EXO 28:43, EXO 30:21; LEV 7:36, LEV 10:9; NUM 10:8

The Levites

NUM 18:23; ; 2CH 8:14

Ordinances in Christian worship

1CO 11:24–11:25 parallels MAT 26:26–26:28, MRK 14:22–14:24, LUK 22:17–22:20 See also MAT 28:19–28:20 Jesus Christ's commands to observe baptism and the Lord's Supper makes them ordinances of Christian worship; ACT 2:38, ACT 16:15, ACT 18:8, ACT 22:16

Ordinances were to be permanent

LEV 3:17 See also EXO 27:21; LEV 23:14, LEV 23:21

Ordinances were to be obeyed

2KI 17:37 See also 1SA 30:25; 2KI 17:34; 2CH 19:10, 2CH 33:8; EZK 44:24

Ordinances were for Israelites and Gentiles alike

NUM 15:15 See also NUM 9:14

God's ordinances are of supreme value

PSA 19:9

title: 7406: Passover
content:
One of the major OT feasts. It specifically commemorates the exodus from Egypt as an act of God's deliverance. In Israel's calendar of feasts it was always celebrated with the Feast of Unleavened Bread.

The institution of the Passover

EXO 12:25–12:27 The Hebrew term “Passover” derives from a verb meaning “to pass over” with the sense of “to spare”.

Celebrating the Passover

Instructions concerning its observance

See also EXO 12:2–12:11, EXO 12:46–12:47; NUM 9:1–9:5; DEU 16:1–16:8

Combined with the Feast of Unleavened Bread

EXO 34:25

The need for ritual cleansing

JHN 11:55

Provision for ritual uncleanness

NUM 9:6–9:13

Provision for non-Jews

EXO 12:43–12:45, EXO 12:48–12:49; ; NUM 9:14

Its observance in the OT

These occasions are times of spiritual renewal, when the nation remembered how the LORD had saved them: JOS 5:10; ; 2KI 23:21–23:23; ; 2CH 30:1–30:5, 2CH 30:13–30:20; ; EZR 6:19–6:21

Ezekiel's vision of the future observance of the Passover

EZK 45:21–45:24

The Passover in the NT

Its observance

LUK 2:41–2:42; ; JHN 6:4; ; ACT 12:4

It was celebrated by Jesus Christ

LUK 22:15 At Passover time Jesus Christ himself was sacrificed and he inaugurated the Lord's Supper. See also MAT 26:17–26:19 parallels MRK 14:12–14:16, LUK 22:7–22:13; JHN 2:23 Jesus Christ observed the Passover like other Jews.

Jesus Christ identified with the Passover lamb

1CO 5:7 See also JHN 1:29, JHN 1:36, JHN 19:36 This may refer to the Passover victim; EXO 12:46; REV 5:5–5:6

title: 7408: Pentecost
content:
One of Israel's three major agricultural festivals, the second great feast of the Jewish year. It is a harvest festival, also known as the Feast of Weeks. The original Jewish significance of this festival has been overshadowed by the Christian celebration of the coming of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost.

Pentecost was one of Israel's three major agricultural festivals

It was also known as the Feast of Weeks

DEU 16:9–16:10 See also EXO 34:22; DEU 16:16–16:17; 2CH 8:13

It is also referred to as the Feast of Harvest

EXO 23:16

Instructions for the celebration of Pentecost

Its timing and sacrifices

LEV 23:15–23:21 The “fifty days” (verse 16) gives rise to the term “Pentecost” which is derived from the Greek word for “fiftieth”; ; NUM 28:26–28:31; ; JER 5:24

Its link with the deliverance from Egypt

DEU 16:12 Pentecost later became associated with covenant renewal and the giving of the Law.

Pentecost in the NT

It was observed by Paul

See also ACT 20:16; 1CO 16:8

Its association with the coming of the Holy Spirit

ACT 2:1–2:4, ACT 2:16–2:21, ACT 11:15 See also JOL 2:28–2:32

title: 7410: phylactery
content:
A leather box containing four sections of the law (Ex 11:13–21) written on parchment, and which Jewish men tied to the forehead and left arm (close to the heart) before morning prayers. The practice of wearing phylacteries arose, probably after the exile, from a literal interpretation by pious Jews of passages such as Ex 13:9.

The significance of wearing phylacteries

A reminder of God's deliverance

EXO 13:11–13:16 See also EXO 13:1–13:10

A reminder of God's requirements

DEU 11:13–11:21 Phylacteries, when worn, were to be an outward sign of the importance of taking God's word into the heart; PSA 119:11; PRO 3:1–3:3 See also DEU 6:4–6:9, DEU 30:14; ROM 10:8; DEU 32:46; PSA 37:31, PSA 40:8; PRO 6:21, PRO 7:2–7:3; JER 31:33

A sign of allegiance and belonging

ISA 44:5; REV 7:3 See also SNG 8:6; REV 9:4, REV 14:1

The practice of wearing phylacteries

MAT 23:5 Jesus Christ condemns the Pharisees for wearing phylacteries for outward show.

title: 7412: priesthood
content: The body of people responsible for priestly duties, especially in relation to worship and sacrifice. The OT identifies Aaron and his heirs as having special priestly duties. In the NT, all believers are understood to share in this priesthood. Jesus Christ fulfils the OT concept of priesthood by being both the high priest and sacrifice, through which forgiveness of sins is completely achieved.

title: 7413: priesthood, in OT
content:
The institution of the priesthood or the particular body of people responsible for priestly duties.

The priesthood as an institution

It was a perpetual institution

EXO 40:15 See also NUM 18:1, NUM 25:13

It was distinct from the laity

NUM 3:10

It was subject to the king's authority

1KI 2:27

It was a privilege

NUM 18:7 See also EXO 29:9; NUM 16:10; JOS 18:7

The priesthood as the body of people responsible for priestly duties

The Aaronic priesthood

EXO 28:41 See also 1CH 6:49; NEH 10:38

The Shiloh priesthood

JOS 18:1 See also 1SA 3:14; 1KI 2:27; PSA 78:60 The Shiloh priesthood ended when the Philistines took Shiloh (see 1Sa 4:17); JER 7:12

The Melchizedek priesthood

PSA 110:4 See also GEN 14:18; HEB 5:10, HEB 7:11–7:17

The Anathoth priesthood

JER 1:1 See also JOS 21:18; 1KI 2:27

The Zadokite priesthood

EZK 40:46 The priestly line of Zadok lasted until 170 B.C.

The Levitical priesthood

DEU 17:9 The term “the priests, who are Levites” appears only in Deuteronomy. Some think it reflects a situation in which all Levites were priests. Not all passages support this view, however, and the phrase may simply distinguish the ministry of the Levitical priests from the priesthood of the king.

title: 7414: priesthood, in NT
content:
Jesus Christ recognised the function of the OT priesthood, but the gospel belief in Christ as high priest and the priesthood of all believers superseded the earlier concept.

Jesus Christ acknowledged the role of the Jerusalem priesthood

See also MAT 8:4 parallels MRK 1:44, LUK 5:14

Characteristics of the Levitical priesthood

It was equivalent to the order of Aaron

HEB 7:11

It was incompatible with membership of the tribe of Judah

HEB 7:14

It was not established by divine oath

HEB 7:20

Characteristics of the Melchizedek priesthood

HEB 7:15–7:16

How Jesus Christ's priesthood resembled the OT priesthood

He fulfilled the requirement of humanity

HEB 2:17 See also HEB 5:1

He was not self-appointed

HEB 5:4–5:5

He resembled Melchizedek

HEB 5:10

How Jesus Christ's priesthood contrasts with the Levitical priesthood

It involves a change in the applicability of the law

HEB 7:12–7:13

It lasts for ever

HEB 7:23–7:24 See also HEB 10:11–10:12

It was based on a once-for-all sacrifice, not of animals but of himself

HEB 9:26 See also HEB 9:12

It is effective, not simply illustrative

HEB 10:14 See also HEB 8:5, HEB 10:1, HEB 10:4

It is not that of a mere man, but of the Son of God

HEB 7:28 See also HEB 4:14, HEB 7:26

Characteristics of the priesthood of all believers

1PE 2:9

They are to reflect the holiness of their great high priest

1PE 1:15 See also HEB 10:10

They are to offer spiritual sacrifices

1PE 2:5 See also ROM 12:1; PHP 4:18; HEB 13:15

They are to intercede for others before God

1TI 2:1 See also REV 5:8, REV 5:10

They are to represent God before other human beings

2CO 5:20 See also EPH 3:7–3:11

resource.label: 7769: priests, types of in NT times

title: 7416: purification
content:
Cleansing from sin or uncleanness, by means of ritual washing under the old covenant, but through the blood of Jesus Christ under the new covenant.

Purification from uncleanness

After contact with something unclean

LEV 11:39–11:40 See also LEV 15:1–15:12, LEV 15:19–15:23, LEV 17:15–17:16; NUM 19:10–19:20

After disease

LEV 15:13–15:15 See also LEV 14:1–14:32

After childbirth and menstruation

LUK 2:22 See also LEV 12:1–12:8; 2SA 11:2–11:4

Purification from sin

1JN 1:7 See also NUM 19:1–19:9; JOB 1:5; HEB 1:3, HEB 9:16–9:24; 1JN 1:9

Purification of people

Priests and Levites

EZR 6:20 See also EXO 30:18–30:21; LEV 16:24–16:28; NUM 8:5–8:22, NUM 19:7; 2CH 4:6; NEH 12:30, NEH 13:22, NEH 13:30

Lay people

ACT 21:24–21:26 See also GEN 35:1–35:2; NUM 19:20, NUM 31:19; 2CH 30:13–30:20; ISA 66:17

Purification of inanimate objects

The temple

2CH 34:8 See also 2CH 29:15–29:19; NEH 13:6–13:9; EZK 45:18–45:20

Sacred objects

EXO 29:36 See also LEV 8:15; 1CH 23:28

Other items

LEV 14:48–14:53 See also NUM 31:19–31:24

Purification of precious metals

PSA 12:6 See also MAL 3:3

Purification of the inner person

2CO 7:1 See also MRK 7:2–7:8; ACT 15:5–15:11; TIT 2:11–2:14; JAS 4:8; 1PE 1:22; 1JN 3:3

title: 7418: Purim
content:
A Jewish festival of rejoicing and feasting celebrated in the month of Adar (February-March) commemorating the deliverance of the Jews recorded in the book of Esther.

The origins of Purim

Esther's position as queen

EST 4:14

Haman's death

EST 7:10

The right of the Jews to avenge themselves on their enemies

EST 8:11

Explanation of the name

EST 9:26 The word “pur”, meaning “lots”, relates to the lots cast to determine the time set for the massacre.

The date for celebrating Purim

See also EST 9:17–9:19 those living in Susa celebrated on 15th Adar, those in rural towns on 14th

The establishment of Purim

EST 9:27, EST 3:7

title: 7420: Rabbi
content:
An honorific title, best translated as “master” or “teacher”, and used to refer to distinguished teachers of the Jewish law. Jesus Christ is called Rabbi in the Gospels, indicating that he was regarded as a respected teacher by his audiences.

John the Baptist's addressed as Rabbi by his disciples

JHN 3:26

Jesus Christ addressed as Rabbi

By two of John the Baptist's disciples

JHN 1:38

By his own disciples

JHN 4:31 See also JHN 6:25, JHN 9:2, JHN 11:8

By Peter

MRK 9:5 See also MRK 11:21

By Nathanael and Nicodemus

JHN 1:49, JHN 3:2

By Judas Iscariot

MAT 26:25 At the Last Supper; MAT 26:49 parallel MRK 14:45 In the Garden of Gethsemane.

Jesus Christ addressed as Rabboni, the Aramaic form of Rabbi

See also MRK 10:51; JHN 20:16

Jesus Christ taught that his disciples were not to be called Rabbi

MAT 23:7–23:8

resource.label: 2203: Jesus Christ, titles and names of

title: 7422: ritual
content:
The regulations governing Jewish religious life and worship, especially sacrifices, ritual cleanliness and food laws. By the sacrificial death of Jesus Christ these rituals have been fulfilled and play no part in the new covenant.

The purpose of ritual sacrifices

To atone for sin

LEV 5:12–5:13 See also EXO 29:38–29:43; LEV 1:3–1:13, LEV 4:1–4:3, LEV 4:13–4:14, LEV 4:20–4:23, LEV 4:26–4:28, LEV 4:31–4:35, LEV 5:13, LEV 6:1–6:7, LEV 7:1–7:7, LEV 16:1–16:34

To maintain fellowship between God and his people

LEV 3:1–3:5, LEV 7:11–7:15 The offering was part burnt and part eaten in a fellowship meal and expressed covenant relationship with God.

To express worship and gratitude to God

The grain offering was the only bloodless offering but it accompanied the other offerings and was burnt whole to express gratitude to God for the harvest and for particular blessings: LEV 2:1–2:3, LEV 2:8–2:16, LEV 6:14–6:23, LEV 7:9–7:10, LEV 9:4; ; NUM 6:14–6:17, NUM 28:3–28:13

Rituals for purification from ritual uncleanness

Childbirth

LUK 2:22–2:24 See also LEV 12:1–12:8

Unclean diseases

MRK 1:40–1:44 parallels MAT 8:2–8:4, LUK 5:12–5:14 See also LEV 14:2–14:7

Unclean discharges

LEV 15:32–15:33

Touching dead bodies

NUM 19:11–19:13

Rituals relating to food

Clean and unclean foods

LEV 11:1–11:2 See also LEV 11:44–11:47

The eating of blood is forbidden

GEN 9:4–9:5; ; LEV 17:11–17:12; ; ACT 15:20

Rituals relating to the priesthood

LEV 8:30 See also EXO 29:1–29:9

Rituals relating to the place of worship

2CH 29:4–29:5 See also LEV 16:15–16:20; 1KI 8:62–8:63 parallel 2CH 7:4–7:5; EZR 6:16–6:17

Rituals relating to special days

Festivals

LEV 23:37

The Sabbath

EXO 31:12–31:17; ; MAT 12:2 parallels MRK 2:24, LUK 6:2

The initiation ritual of circumcision

GEN 17:10–17:14

Ritual only has meaning when accompanied by obedience

ROM 2:25 See also ISA 1:11–1:17; MAT 15:16–15:20 parallel MRK 7:18–7:23; MAT 23:25; ROM 2:28–2:29; 1CO 7:19, 1CO 8:8

Rituals of the old covenant are fulfilled by Jesus Christ

COL 2:17 See also MRK 2:27–2:28; COL 2:11; HEB 10:1–10:3, HEB 10:8–10:10

Old covenant rituals are abolished under the new covenant

HEB 9:10 See also MRK 7:19; ACT 10:9–10:15; ROM 7:6, ROM 14:14, ROM 14:20; GAL 5:6, GAL 6:15; EPH 2:15; COL 2:13–2:16

title: 7424: ritual law
content:
The rules and regulations regarding the conduct of the priests and people of Israel in approaching God, especially in worship. They were to be holy as God is holy. The perfect sacrifice of Jesus Christ on the cross is seen by NT writers as fulfilling this demand for holiness and obedience.

The ritual law and the priesthood

The priests were to be careful to obey God's commandments

LEV 22:9 See also LEV 10:10, LEV 22:3

The priests had to avoid ceremonial uncleanness

Contact with the dead caused uncleanness and was forbidden to the high priest: LEV 21:1–21:3, LEV 21:11; ; NUM 19:11, NUM 19:14; LEV 21:16–21:23 No-one with a physical defect could serve as a priest; ; LEV 22:20–22:25 The sacrifices offered by the priests also had to be without defect.

The ritual law and the people of Israel

The people were called to holiness

LEV 19:2, LEV 20:26 See also LEV 11:44–11:45, LEV 20:7, LEV 21:8, LEV 22:32

The ritual law stipulated what they could eat

LEV 11:46–11:47 See also GEN 7:2, GEN 8:20; LEV 11:1–11:23 parallel DEU 14:3–14:20; DEU 14:21

The ritual law regarding childbirth

LEV 12:1–12:5; ; LUK 2:22–2:24

Jesus Christ's obedience and perfect sacrifice on the cross fulfilled the demands of the ritual law

HEB 10:8–10:10 See also EPH 2:15; COL 2:14; HEB 7:11, HEB 7:18, HEB 7:27, HEB 10:1–10:3, HEB 10:11–10:14

resource.label: 8269: holiness, as separation from the worldly

title: 7426: ritual washing
content:
The act of washing for consecration or for purification from uncleanness. It involved washing all or part of the body or one's clothing. Jesus Christ's attitude to the Pharisees reflected, not a disavowal of ritual washing, but disapproval of their emphasis on the outward, rather than inward, forms of religion.

Ritual washing for consecration

EXO 19:10–19:11 See also EXO 19:14, EXO 40:12–40:15; NUM 8:5–8:7, NUM 8:21–8:22

Ritual washing for cleansing

LEV 17:15 See also LEV 13:6, LEV 13:34, LEV 15:16–15:18, LEV 15:21–15:23; NUM 19:10, NUM 19:19

Methods of ritual washing

Washing clothes

NUM 31:24 See also LEV 6:27, LEV 11:24–11:28, LEV 11:39–11:40, LEV 13:58, LEV 14:43–14:47; NUM 19:21; REV 7:14, REV 22:14

Bathing

LEV 14:8–14:9 See also LEV 15:4–15:13, LEV 15:27, LEV 16:26–16:28, LEV 17:15; NUM 19:7–19:8; DEU 23:9–23:11; 2KI 5:9–5:14; PSA 51:7; HEB 10:19–10:22

Sprinkling with the water of purification

NUM 19:17–19:19 See also NUM 8:5–8:7, NUM 19:13; EZK 36:24–36:25

Washing hands and feet

EXO 30:17–30:21 See also EXO 40:30–40:32; DEU 21:6–21:9; JHN 13:5–13:10; JAS 4:8

Consequences of the failure to perform ritual washing

NUM 19:20 See also LEV 17:15–17:16; NUM 19:13

The NT perception of ritual washing

It was practised in NT times

JHN 2:6 See also JHN 3:25

The Pharisees over-emphasised its importance

MAT 15:1–15:11 parallel MRK 7:1–7:23 See also LUK 11:37–11:41

Jesus Christ's atoning work rendered it unnecessary

HEB 9:6–9:14

title: 7428: Sabbath
content: The day of rest laid down for the people of God. The OT treated the seventh day of the week (Saturday) as the Sabbath, a custom continued in modern Judaism. The Christian church, in recognition of the importance of the resurrection of Jesus Christ, observed a day of rest on the first day of the week (Sunday).

title: 7429: Sabbath, in OT
content:
The Sabbath of rest is grounded in God's work of creation. Observance of a Sabbath day is distinctive of the people of God.

The Sabbath grounded in creation itself

GEN 2:3 See also PSA 118:24

The purpose of the Sabbath

To remember God's work in creation

EXO 20:8–20:11 See also GEN 2:2; EXO 35:2

To remember the exodus

DEU 5:12–5:15 The idea of rest relates the concept of the Sabbath to the ultimate conquest of Canaan under David and to the return from exile. See also GEN 8:4; 2SA 7:1, 2SA 7:11; PSA 95:10–95:11; HEB 4:9; REV 14:13

To be a sign of the relationship between Israel and God and to give refreshment

EXO 31:17 See also DEU 5:12–5:14

The Law required the Sabbath to be a holy day free from work

LEV 23:3 See also EXO 34:21, EXO 35:3; LEV 23:38; ISA 56:2, ISA 58:13

The Sabbath was linked with celebration of the New Moon

2KI 4:23 See also ISA 1:13; EZK 46:3; HOS 2:11; AMO 8:5

Abuses of the Sabbath

EXO 16:27–16:28 gathering manna; ; NUM 15:32 gathering wood; ; NEH 13:15–13:18 doing ordinary work; NEH 10:31; ; AMO 8:5; JER 17:21 carrying loads

Punishments for infringing the Sabbath law

The death penalty

EXO 31:14; ; NUM 15:35

Disaster for Jerusalem

JER 17:27; ; EZK 20:13, EZK 22:8, EZK 22:15

Sacrifices to be offered on the Sabbath

Bread

LEV 24:8; ; 1CH 9:32

Burnt offerings

NUM 28:9–28:10; ; 1CH 23:31; ; 2CH 2:4

Other offerings

EZK 46:4

title: 7430: Sabbath, in NT
content:
The NT develops the OT teaching on the Sabbath in three important directions. It declares that the Sabbath should not be observed in a legalistic manner; the Sabbath-rest is treated as an important symbol of the Christian doctrine of salvation; and finally, the NT itself indicates how Sunday, rather than Saturday, came to be seen as the Christian Sabbath.

Gospel incidents connected with the Sabbath

Exorcism

MRK 1:21–1:25 parallel LUK 4:31–4:35

Healing

MAT 12:9–12:14 parallels MRK 3:1–3:6, LUK 6:6–6:11; ; MRK 1:30–1:31 parallel LUK 4:38–4:40; ; LUK 13:10–13:17, LUK 14:1–14:6; ; JHN 5:5–5:18, JHN 9:1–9:16

Teaching

MRK 6:2 parallel MAT 13:54; ; LUK 4:16

Other references

MAT 28:1 parallel MRK 16:1; ; LUK 23:55–23:56; ; JHN 12:2

Jesus Christ's teaching regarding the Sabbath

Jesus Christ observes the Sabbath regulation

LUK 4:16 See also MAT 24:20; ACT 1:12

Human well-being is more important than rigid observance of the Law

MRK 2:27–2:28 See also MAT 12:3

Ceremonial observance must give way before any higher, or more spiritual, motive

MAT 12:5–12:6 See also LUK 6:5

Sabbath reading of Scripture provided an opportunity for reaching the Jews

ACT 17:2 See also ACT 13:14, ACT 13:27, ACT 13:42, ACT 13:44, ACT 15:21, ACT 16:13, ACT 18:4

Sabbath observance was optional for Gentile Christians

COL 2:16

The Lord's Day

REV 1:10 At an early stage the Sabbath was replaced by Sunday (the first day of the week) as the day for rest and worship. See also JHN 20:19, JHN 20:26; ACT 20:7; 1CO 16:2

The Sabbath-rest is seen as a symbol of the salvation of the people of God

HEB 4:1 The OT promise of rest was unfulfilled, on account of Israel's disobedience; HEB 3:18–3:19, HEB 4:9 The OT promise of rest remains open.

title: 7431: Sabbath, sabbatical year
content:
Just as the seventh day was observed as a day of rest, so the OT regarded every seventh year as a sabbath year, to be set aside for rest.

The sabbath year

As a time for leaving fields fallow

EXO 23:10–23:11 See also LEV 25:4, LEV 26:34; NEH 10:31 An important feature was the opportunity for the poor to benefit from unharvested fields.

As a time for cancelling debts

DEU 15:1 See also DEU 15:9, DEU 31:10; NEH 10:31

As a time for freeing slaves

DEU 15:12 See also JER 34:14

The Year of Jubilee as seven Sabbaths

LEV 25:8–25:12

title: 7434: sacrifice
content: An important aspect of the relationship between God and humanity but whereas the OT describes many sacrifices, the NT announces the fulfilment of sacrifice in Jesus Christ.

title: 7435: sacrifice, in OT
content:
An act that involved offering to God the life of an animal. It expressed gratitude for God's goodness or acknowledgment of sin. It was also associated with establishing a covenant.

Sacrifice was an integral part of worship

See also GEN 46:1; EXO 10:24–10:26; JDG 13:19; 1SA 1:3; 1KI 3:4

Sacrifices were a means of offering thanks to God

GEN 4:4 Abel; ; GEN 8:20 Noah delivered from the flood; ; EXO 18:12 Jethro after God had delivered the Israelites; ; JDG 11:31 Jephthah; ; 1SA 6:15 after the safe return of the ark; EZR 3:3, EZR 8:35; PSA 27:6, PSA 54:6, PSA 107:17–107:22

Sacrifices were offered at regular religious festivals

LEV 23:5–23:8 Passover; ; LEV 23:18–23:20 the Feast of Weeks; ; LEV 23:23–23:25 the Feast of Trumpets; LEV 16:6–16:10, LEV 23:26–23:32; LEV 23:33–23:36 the Feast of Tabernacles

Special occasions were marked by sacrifices

1KI 8:63 the dedication of Solomon's temple; ; 2CH 29:31–29:33 after Hezekiah had purified the temple; ; EZR 3:2–3:3 the Jews who returned from the Babylonian exile; ; EZR 6:17 at the completion of the second temple

Sacrifices as signs of individual and national penitence
