LEV 4:1–4:3, LEV 4:13–4:14; ; JDG 2:1–2:5, JDG 20:26; ; 1SA 7:8–7:9; ; 2SA 24:10–24:25

The place of sacrifice

It was divinely chosen

DEU 12:13–12:14 See also LEV 17:3–17:5; DEU 12:2–12:6

Rival places of sacrifice caused the Israelites to sin

1KI 12:28–12:29, 1KI 12:32; ; 2CH 15:17, 2CH 31:1 Under Hezekiah the high places were destroyed.

God condemned certain sacrifices

Sacrifices to other gods

1KI 11:7–11:8 See also NUM 25:1–25:3; 2KI 16:4, 2KI 16:15; PSA 106:28; ISA 57:7, ISA 65:3, ISA 65:7; JER 19:4

Human sacrifices

LEV 18:21; ; DEU 12:31; ; 1KI 16:34; ; 2KI 3:26–3:27, 2KI 16:3, 2KI 17:31, 2KI 21:6; ; EZK 20:31

Sacrifice to the LORD may be rejected

If it is a substitute for obedience

1SA 15:20–15:22; ; JER 7:21–7:22; ; HOS 8:11–8:13; ; AMO 4:4; ; MRK 12:33

If it is a substitute for justice and mercy

ISA 66:2–66:3; ; MIC 6:7–6:8; ; MAT 9:13

If it is imperfect

MAL 1:13–1:14

Blessing is promised when right sacrifices are offered

GEN 22:15–22:18; ; PSA 4:5, PSA 50:14–50:15, PSA 50:23, PSA 51:17

title: 7436: sacrifice, NT fulfilment of
content:
For the people of the new covenant, sacrifice is fulfilled in Jesus Christ. Christians should have nothing to do with other sacrifices but are to bring their own “spiritual” offerings.

The OT points ahead to the fulfilment of sacrifice in Jesus Christ

A new sacrificial system

ISA 56:6–56:7 See also ISA 19:21

The new people of God

JER 33:18; ; EZK 20:40

The vision of the new temple

EZK 40:46

The perfect sacrifice of Jesus Christ

Jesus Christ perceived his death as a sacrifice

MAT 26:28 parallels MRK 14:24, LUK 22:20, 1CO 11:25 See also HEB 5:1

The contrast with the OT sacrificial system

HEB 7:27 See also HEB 9:23–9:26, HEB 10:1–10:3, HEB 10:12

Jesus Christ's sacrifice of a life dedicated to God makes believers holy

TIT 2:14; ; HEB 10:8–10:10

The sacrifices of Christians

Christians offer themselves as living sacrifices

ROM 12:1 PHP 2:17; ; 2TI 4:6

The sacrifice of praise and good works

HEB 13:15–13:16

Christian attitudes to pagan sacrifice

See also 1CO 10:18–10:22, 1CO 10:25–10:26, 1CO 10:27–10:29 Christians must not appear to compromise with idolatrous worship.

title: 7438: sanctuary
content:
A place or structure set aside for the worship of God. In Scripture the word generally refers to the tabernacle or the Jerusalem temple. By extension the word signifies a location or spiritual state providing refuge from hostile forces, human, spiritual or natural.

The tabernacle

EXO 25:8–25:9 See also EXO 36:1; HEB 8:2, HEB 8:5

Worship in the tabernacle was divinely ordained in great detail

EXO 30:7–30:10; ; LEV 7:37–7:38, LEV 23:44; ; EXO 39:1; ; NUM 3:30–3:31; ; HEB 9:1

The people gave generously for the tabernacle

EXO 36:4–36:5

Approach to God's sanctuary was strictly regulated

LEV 12:4 See also LEV 21:21–21:23; NUM 3:10, NUM 3:38, NUM 8:19, NUM 18:5, NUM 19:20; 2CH 26:18

A reverent attitude was commanded

LEV 19:30 parallel LEV 26:2

The Jerusalem temple

David made extensive preparations for Solomon to build the temple

1CH 22:17–22:19 See also 1CH 28:2–28:3, 1CH 28:6

The inner sanctuary of the temple corresponded to the Most Holy Place in the tabernacle

1KI 6:19 See also 1KI 8:6 parallel 2CH 5:7; 1CH 6:49; HEB 9:1–9:4

Some places were considered sacred because of God's presence and activity

EXO 15:17 See also GEN 28:16–28:17; EXO 3:1–3:5; PSA 15:1, PSA 63:2, PSA 73:16–73:17; JER 17:12

Conduct that displeases God will lead to a nation's sanctuaries being destroyed

Disobedience to God

LEV 26:31 See also 2CH 30:8; EZK 7:24; LAM 2:7; AMO 7:9; MAL 2:11

Dishonesty in dealings

EZK 28:18

Idolatry

See also LEV 20:3; EZK 8:6, EZK 23:38–23:39

Profaning the sanctuary

EZK 44:7–44:8; ; ZEP 3:4

Sanctuary from vengeance: the cities of refuge

JOS 20:2–20:3 See also NUM 35:6, NUM 35:9–35:12

God himself is his people's sanctuary

PSA 31:20 See also DEU 33:27; PSA 17:7–17:9, PSA 27:1, PSA 32:7, PSA 46:1, PSA 64:1–64:2, PSA 91:4; ISA 25:4; EZK 11:16; ROM 8:38–8:39; PHP 4:7; HEB 6:19

Non-material ``sanctuaries'' of God

Heaven, where God is perfectly worshipped

PSA 102:19 See also HEB 9:24

God's worshipping people, in whom God dwells

PSA 114:2 See also 1CO 6:19; EPH 2:21; 1PE 2:5

Jesus Christ himself as the ``place'' where God dwelt on earth

JHN 2:19–2:21

title: 7440: scapegoat
content:
A goat which was sent into the wilderness by the high priest, as a symbol of the removal of the sins of the people.

The scapegoat as part of an OT ritual

LEV 16:7–16:10, LEV 16:20–16:22 See also LEV 16:26; ISA 53:4

The scapegoat anticipates Jesus Christ's atonement

JHN 1:29; ; 2CO 5:21; ; HEB 9:11–9:14 Jesus Christ's death is here contrasted with the OT sacrificial system.

title: 7442: shrine
content:
A local sanctuary, often dedicated to pagan worship. Before the building of the temple the Israelites worshipped at several divinely sanctioned shrines; afterwards, worship at a central sanctuary was required. True worship of God required that pagan shrines be destroyed. It was Israel's failure to abandon these religious sites that led to syncretism and apostasy.

Common features of pagan shrines

A location on high ground under trees

EZK 6:13 Mountains were considered the dwelling- places of the gods, whilst trees were associated with fertility. See also 2KI 17:10; ISA 1:29 sacred trees; JER 2:20; EZK 18:11 mountain shrines; EZK 20:28

Idols and sacred symbols

ISA 44:13 See also 2KI 10:26–10:27, 2KI 17:29–17:31; 2CH 34:3–34:4; ACT 19:24

A serving priesthood

2KI 23:5, 2KI 23:20

Sacrifice to the deity

JER 48:35 See also 1KI 22:43; 2KI 12:3, 2KI 16:4 parallel 2CH 28:4; 2CH 28:25

Shrine-prostitution

GEN 38:21–38:22; ; DEU 23:17; ; 1KI 14:24, 1KI 15:12; ; JOB 36:14

Pagan shrines were to be destroyed

On Israel's entry into Canaan

DEU 12:2–12:3 See also EXO 34:13–34:14; NUM 33:52; DEU 7:5

As a sign of religious reformation

JDG 6:25; 2KI 23:8–23:20 See also 2KI 10:25; 2CH 14:3, 2CH 17:6; 2KI 18:4

Shrines in Israel for the worship of God

Worship at the high places

1KI 3:2–3:3 Following Canaanite custom, Israel set up shrines on high ground possibly, against God's instruction, at old Baal sites. Worship at these non-legitimate sites was condemned. See also 2CH 33:17

Worship at sites sanctioned by God

EXO 20:24 See also JDG 6:22–6:24; 1SA 9:12–9:14, 1SA 10:5

Examples of shrines dedicated to God

Bethel: GEN 12:8; ; JDG 21:2; JOS 18:1; ; JDG 21:19; JDG 20:1; ; 1SA 7:6; 1KI 3:4–3:5; ; 1CH 16:39–16:40; ; 2CH 1:13

A central place of worship was later required

2KI 18:22 parallel ISA 36:7 Following the building of the temple, worship in various scattered shrines was condemned, probably because of associations with pagan worship. See also DEU 12:4–12:7 The central sanctuary was, initially, the place where the tabernacle was located. Eventually this was superseded by the temple; 2SA 7:13 parallel 1CH 17:12

Israel's failure to abandon worship at the shrines led to apostasy

Improper worship at shrines dedicated to God

JDG 17:5 See also JDG 18:30–18:31; 1KI 12:28–12:31, 1KI 13:32; 2KI 17:32–17:33; 2CH 11:15; AMO 4:4

The corruption of temple worship

2KI 23:4–23:7; ; EZK 8:14–8:16

Shrines dedicated to false gods

1KI 14:23–14:24; EZK 8:12 See also 1KI 11:7–11:8; 2KI 21:2–21:3JER 7:31, JER 19:5, JER 32:35 AMO 5:26; ACT 7:43

Israel's spiritual adultery

EZK 16:24–16:25 See also JER 3:6; EZK 16:39, EZK 18:6; HOS 4:12–4:13

God's judgment on false places of worship

EZK 6:3–6:6 See also LEV 26:30–26:31; HOS 10:8

title: 7444: sin offering
content:
Offerings to make atonement for both moral and ritual offences. The NT sees them as foreshadowing the death of Jesus Christ as an offering for human sin.

Occasions when a sin offering was required

Unintentional sins against God's laws: LEV 4:2; ; HEB 9:7; LEV 5:1–5:4, LEV 4:20 The consequence of the offering is atonement and forgiveness.; LEV 12:6–12:8; ; LUK 2:24; LEV 14:13–14:31; ; MRK 1:44; LEV 15:15, LEV 15:30

The form the sin offering took depended on who committed the sin

LEV 4:3 a priest who sins; ; LEV 4:14 the sin of the community as a whole; ; LEV 4:22–4:23 a leader who sins unintentionally; LEV 4:27–4:35, LEV 5:5–5:6

The ceremony when presenting an animal as a sin offering

The guilty identify themselves with the offering before killing it

LEV 4:4, LEV 4:15, LEV 4:24

Confession

LEV 5:5

Blood, representing the victim's lifeblood, is put on the altar

LEV 4:25, LEV 4:30, LEV 4:34

The best parts, being burned on the altar, are offered to God

LEV 4:8–4:10, LEV 4:26, LEV 4:35

Male members of priestly families may eat what remains

LEV 6:26, LEV 6:29, LEV 10:16–10:20

Special procedures for the offerings of the poor

LEV 5:7–5:13

The special ceremony for priests and the community presenting a sin offering

Blood is taken into the sanctuary and placed on the altar

LEV 4:5–4:7, LEV 4:16–4:18

The rest of the animal is incinerated, not eaten

LEV 4:11–4:12, LEV 4:21 emphasising the identity between the victim and the sin of the community

Significant occasions when sin offerings were made

LEV 8:14–8:17 at the ordination of Aaron and his sons; ; LEV 16:1–16:34 on the Day of Atonement; ; 2KI 12:16 during Josiah's reformation; ; 2CH 29:21–29:24 at the beginning of Hezekiah's reign; ; EZR 6:17 at the dedication of the second temple; ; EZR 8:35 when the exiles returned; EZK 40:39, EZK 42:13, EZK 43:19, EZK 43:21

The NT perspective on the sin offering

Jesus Christ was the supreme sin offering

ROM 8:3; 2CO 5:21 NIV footnote.

Animal sacrifices had limited value

See also HEB 9:7–9:10

The death of Jesus Christ brings full cleansing and forgiveness

HEB 9:11–9:14 See also 1PE 1:18–1:19

Unlike earthly priests, Jesus Christ needed no sin offering

HEB 7:27 See also HEB 5:1–5:3

title: 7446: slavery
content: The state of being an unpaid servant, whether through forcible subjection or voluntary submission. Slavery was common in biblical times, and Scripture lays down strict guidelines as to how slaves are to be treated by their masters, stressing the new status that slaves have as a result of being believers in Jesus Christ. The image of slavery is also used to describe the state of sinful human beings.

title: 7447: slavery, in OT
content:
Slavery in the OT occurs when someone either voluntarily or forcibly becomes completely subject to another person's control.

Reasons for slavery

War

DEU 20:10–20:11 See also GEN 14:21; NUM 31:9; 2KI 5:2; 2CH 28:8

Purchase

LEV 25:44–25:45 See also GEN 37:23–37:28

Debt

2KI 4:1 See also EXO 22:2–22:3; LEV 25:39, LEV 25:47–25:48; DEU 15:12; NEH 5:1–5:5

Birth

EXO 21:4 See also GEN 17:12–17:13; ECC 2:7; JER 2:14

Conscription of foreigners as forced labour

2SA 12:29–12:31 See also EXO 1:11–1:14; JOS 9:22–9:23, JOS 16:10; JDG 1:28; 1KI 9:20–9:21

Rights of slaves

Rights of protection against physical and sexual abuse

LEV 25:43 See also EXO 21:7–21:11, EXO 21:20–21:21, EXO 21:26–21:27

Rights to participate in religious feasts and festivals

EXO 23:12 See also EXO 20:9–20:10; DEU 12:11–12:12

Rights to inherit property

GEN 15:3

Rights of asylum

DEU 23:15

The release of slaves

Release in the Year of Jubilee

LEV 25:54–25:55 See also LEV 25:39–25:41

Release after six years of service

EXO 21:2 See also EXO 21:3–21:6; DEU 15:12–15:18; JER 34:8–34:20 The six-year principle was being abused in the time of Jeremiah.

Release through redemption by a near relative

LEV 25:47–25:53

The status of slaves

Slaves as property

LEV 25:46 See also EXO 21:32; LEV 25:39–25:42 Fellow Israelites were not to be regarded as property.

Slaves as trusted advisors

1SA 9:5–9:10

title: 7448: slavery, in NT
content:
The NT does not condone slavery but recognises that slavery exists, and that many slaves have become believers. The status of slaves in the church is transformed by the work of Jesus Christ. Slaves were accorded greater respect because they were seen to be spiritually equal with others.

Examples of slavery in the NT

ACT 16:16–16:21 Paul and Silas deliver a demon-possessed slave girl; ; PHM 1:8–1:21 Paul returns the runaway slave Onesimus to his master.

The gospel transforms the status of slaves

1CO 12:13; GAL 3:26–3:28

Instructions concerning slavery

Instructions to Christian slaves

EPH 6:5–6:8 See also 1CO 7:20–7:24; COL 3:22–3:24; 1TI 6:1–6:2; TIT 2:9–2:10; 1PE 2:18–2:21

Instructions to Christian masters

EPH 6:9 See also COL 4:1; PHM 1:8–1:17

The immorality of trading in slaves

1TI 1:9–1:10

title: 7449: slavery, spiritual
content:
Because of sin humanity is in a state of slavery. On account of the atoning work of Jesus Christ, believers can break free from slavery to sin and enter into the glorious liberty of the children of God.

Humanity is enslaved

To sin

JHN 8:33–8:36 See also ROM 6:12–6:18, ROM 7:7–7:25; TIT 3:3; 2PE 2:19

To the law

GAL 5:1 See also GAL 4:1–4:7

To fear

ROM 8:15 See also HEB 2:14–2:15

Jesus Christ as the example of the true slave

JHN 13:1–13:17 Washing feet was the responsibility of the slaves of the household. See also MAT 20:25–20:28 parallel MRK 10:42–10:45; PHP 2:5–2:11

Believers as slaves of God

They are God's servants

ROM 6:22 See also MAT 6:24 parallel LUK 16:13; ROM 1:1; COL 3:23–3:24; JAS 1:1; 1PE 2:16; 2PE 1:1

They are sons and heirs

GAL 4:1–4:7 See also ROM 8:12–8:17

They have friendship with Jesus Christ

JHN 15:15 See also JAS 2:21–2:23

They have true freedom

JHN 8:31–8:36 See also GAL 4:21–4:31

Believers as slaves of righteousness

ROM 6:15–6:23

Believers as slaves of others

MAT 20:25–20:28 parallel MRK 10:42–10:45 See also 1CO 9:19; 2CO 4:5; GAL 5:13

The image of slavery in the teaching of Jesus Christ

MAT 24:45–24:51 parallel LUK 12:42–12:46 Slavery involves readiness for the Master's return; ; MAT 25:14–25:20 parallel LUK 19:12–19:17 Slavery involves faithfulness to the Master's purpose; ; LUK 17:7–17:10 Slavery involves diligence for the Master's good.

Creation is itself in slavery until Jesus Christ returns

ROM 8:19–8:22

resource.label: 2363: Jesus Christ, preaching and teaching of

title: 7452: snake, bronze
content:
The means of salvation for Israel in the wilderness, a parallel to be found in Jesus Christ.

Healing and salvation resulted from looking at the bronze snake

NUM 21:8–21:9 See also NUM 21:4–21:7

The bronze snake became an object of idolatrous worship

2KI 18:4

The bronze snake parallels Jesus Christ's crucifixion

JHN 3:14–3:15

title: 7454: sprinkling
content:
In the OT, the blood of a sacrificial animal was sprinkled against the altar as a sign of atonement or consecration to the LORD. The NT uses the same image to refer to the gaining of forgiveness through the sacrificial death of Jesus Christ.

Sprinkling of an animal's blood as a sign of atonement

On the altar

LEV 7:2 See also EXO 24:6; LEV 1:5, LEV 1:11, LEV 3:2, LEV 3:8, LEV 3:13, LEV 5:9, LEV 9:12, LEV 9:18, LEV 17:6; NUM 18:17; 2KI 16:13, 2KI 16:15; 2CH 29:22–29:24, 2CH 30:16, 2CH 35:11; EZK 43:18, EZK 43:20; HEB 9:21

In front of the inner curtain in the tabernacle

LEV 4:6 See also LEV 4:17

Towards the front of the Tent of Meeting

NUM 19:4

On the front of the atonement cover

LEV 16:14–16:16

On the door-frames of the houses where the Passover lamb was eaten

EXO 12:7 See also EXO 12:22; HEB 11:28

Sprinkling of blood as a sign of consecration

The consecration of people

EXO 24:8 See also EXO 29:20–29:21; LEV 8:23; HEB 9:19

The consecration of the altar

LEV 16:18–16:19

Sprinkling of oil as a sign of consecration

LEV 8:11 See also LEV 8:30, LEV 14:15–14:16, LEV 14:27

Sprinkling (usually with water) as a sign of cleansing

NUM 8:7; EZK 36:25 See also LEV 14:7, LEV 14:51; NUM 19:13, NUM 19:18–19:21; ISA 52:15; HEB 9:13

Sprinkling as an image of forgiveness in Christ

1PE 1:2 See also HEB 9:11–9:14, HEB 9:18–9:28, HEB 10:22, HEB 12:24

Sprinkling of dust as a visible sign of mourning

JOS 7:6 See also JOB 2:12; LAM 2:10; EZK 27:30

title: 7456: synagogue
content:
A word meaning literally “a gathering together”. In the NT it sometimes refers to a group of Jews meeting for worship but most often to the building in which they met.

Synagogue worship may have begun during the exile

EZK 14:1 Unable to sacrifice in the temple, the community met together with spiritual leaders. See also EZK 20:1

A synagogue might be established anywhere

MRK 1:21 See also ACT 6:9 in JerusalemACT 16:12–16:13, ACT 16:16 ACT 17:1, ACT 17:10, ACT 18:19

The role of the synagogue

It was a focus for prayer and the reading of Scripture

ACT 13:15 See also NEH 8:2–8:8, NEH 9:3; LUK 4:15–4:33

It was a centre for community affairs

MAT 10:17; ; MRK 13:9; ; LUK 12:11

Exclusion from the synagogue was used as a punishment

JHN 9:22, JHN 12:42

The leading of synagogue worship

It was the responsibility of the synagogue ruler

MRK 5:22; ; ACT 18:8

Qualified laymen were allowed to teach

LUK 4:16 See also ACT 13:15

Attendance at the synagogue

It was the practice of Jesus Christ

MAT 9:35 See also MRK 6:2; LUK 4:16; JHN 6:59

It was the practice of Paul and other Jewish Christians

ACT 9:19–9:20 See also ACT 14:1–14:2, ACT 17:2, ACT 19:8

The first Christians continued to worship in the synagogues

LUK 24:53; ; ACT 19:8–19:9

resource.label: 2363: Jesus Christ, preaching and teaching of

title: 7458: tabernacle, the
content: The tabernacle (tent) was a portable and temporary shrine used for worship before the temple was built. It denotes the temporary dwelling of God among his people.

title: 7459: tabernacle, in OT
content:
A prefabricated portable structure that was used for worship from the wilderness period until Solomon built the Jerusalem temple.

The tabernacle was the place of God's presence

EXO 33:9–33:10 See also EXO 25:8, EXO 40:34–40:35

The construction of the tabernacle

It was built at God's command

EXO 25:9 See also EXO 25:40, EXO 39:32, EXO 39:43

The materials used in its construction

EXO 25:3–25:7 Many of these materials were available in the desert. Others, such as gold, would have been obtained from the Egyptians (Ex 12:35–36); ; EXO 35:21–35:24

The plan for its construction

EXO 26:1, EXO 26:7, EXO 26:14–26:15, EXO 26:26

The skilled labour employed in its construction

EXO 31:1–31:6 parallel EXO 35:30–35:35 See also EXO 35:10, EXO 35:26, EXO 36:1–36:2

The tabernacle courtyard and its contents

Its dimensions and construction

EXO 27:9, EXO 27:12, EXO 27:18

The altar of burnt offering

EXO 27:1

The laver

EXO 30:17–30:18

The tabernacle's Holy Place and its contents

Its construction

EXO 26:36–26:37

The incense altar

EXO 30:1, EXO 30:5

The lampstand

EXO 25:31

The golden table for the bread of the Presence

EXO 25:23, EXO 25:30, EXO 26:35

The tabernacle's Most Holy Place and its contents

It was separated from the Holy Place by a curtain

EXO 26:31–26:33 It was designed as a perfect cube with sides of 10 cubits (4.5 metres).

It contained the ark of the covenant

EXO 26:33–26:34

The Levites were responsible for the tabernacle

NUM 1:50–1:51

The non-religious functions of the tabernacle

As a reference point for locating the different tribes in camp

NUM 2:1–2:2 Symbolising that God was central to the life of the nation.

The movement of the cloud of glory showed when it was time to make or break camp

See also EXO 40:36–40:38

Places where the tabernacle was sited

The tabernacle was dedicated at Sinai: EXO 40:1–40:2, EXO 40:9–40:11; JOS 18:1; ; 1SA 1:3; ; PSA 78:60; 1SA 21:1 Nob; ; 1CH 16:39 Gibeon; ; 2CH 5:2–5:6 Jerusalem

title: 7460: tabernacle, in NT
content:
The NT sees the tabernacle and its ceremonies as symbolising the saving work of Jesus Christ in his incarnation and death and in the experience of believers.

Jesus Christ as the tabernacle where God dwells

JHN 1:14, JHN 2:19–2:21

The tabernacle showed that God was not limited to one place

See also ACT 7:44–7:47

The spiritual significance of the tabernacle

HEB 9:1–9:5

The earthly tabernacle as a copy of the heavenly one

HEB 8:5 See also HEB 8:1–8:2

The altar

REV 6:9–6:10, REV 8:3–8:5

The ark of the covenant

REV 11:19

The atonement cover

EXO 25:22; ; HEB 4:16

The curtain preventing access to the holiest place

MAT 27:51; ; HEB 6:19–6:20, HEB 9:8, HEB 10:19–10:20

Jesus Christ as high priest

Cleansing the earthly tabernacle

HEB 9:21 See also HEB 9:6–9:8 on the Day of Atonement; HEB 9:25

Through the blood of the cross, Jesus Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary

HEB 9:24 See also HEB 9:28

title: 7462: tassel
content:
A fringe of twisted threads attached to the bottom edge of Israelite men's cloaks. The Pharisees lengthened the tassels on their cloaks to draw attention to their piety.

Tassels attached to outer garments

On the corners of garments

NUM 15:38 See also DEU 22:12

On the cloak of Jesus Christ

MAT 9:20 parallels MRK 5:27, LUK 8:44 The woman probably touched the tassels on Jesus Christ's cloak. See also MAT 14:36 parallel MRK 6:56

Tassels as a reminder of God's law

NUM 15:39–15:41 The movement of the tassels would draw back wandering eyes to God and his law.

Ostentatious tassels symbolise outward religious show

MAT 23:5–23:7 parallels MRK 12:38–12:39, LUK 20:46 See also MAT 6:1–6:6

title: 7464: teachers of the law
content:
Those professionally trained to develop, teach and apply the OT law. In the application of the law the oral teaching of these people often assumed greater authority than the written law. Thus, by the time of the NT, the teachers of the law were in conflict with Jesus Christ and the apostles, who taught with authority and condemned the outward religious acts that the teachers had fostered.

Teachers of the law mentioned by name

Ezra

EZR 7:1–7:21; ; NEH 8:1–8:18 Teachers of the law arose after the exile from amongst the earlier scribes whose duties were more strictly secretarial.

Nicodemus

JHN 3:10

Gamaliel

ACT 5:34 Teachers of the law belonged mainly to the Pharisees but as a body were distinct from them.

Teachers of the law in conflict with Jesus Christ

Concerning righteousness

MAT 5:20

Concerning Jesus Christ's authority

MAT 9:2–9:8 parallels MRK 2:5–2:12, LUK 5:20–5:26 See also MRK 3:22–3:27, MRK 11:27–11:33 parallel LUK 20:1–20:8

Concerning the seeking of signs

MAT 12:38–12:39

Concerning hypocrisy

MAT 23:2–23:33 parallels MRK 12:38–12:40, LUK 13:34–13:35, LUK 20:46 See also MAT 15:1–15:8 parallel MRK 7:1–7:8; ISA 29:13

Concerning the children's praise

MAT 21:15–21:16 See also PSA 8:2

Concerning Jesus Christ's mixing with sinners

MRK 2:16–2:17 parallel LUK 5:30–5:32 See also LUK 15:1–15:7

Concerning Jesus Christ's identity

MRK 12:35–12:37 See also PSA 110:1

Concerning the Sabbath

LUK 6:7–6:11

The teachers of the law sought to get rid of Jesus Christ

By falsely accusing him

LUK 23:10 See also MAT 26:57–26:60 parallel MRK 14:53–14:56 The teachers of the law were entrusted with the administration of the law as judges in the Sanhedrin; JHN 8:3–8:6

By looking for ways to arrest and kill him

MRK 11:18 parallel LUK 19:47 See also MRK 14:1 parallel LUK 22:2; MRK 14:43; LUK 20:19 parallel MRK 12:12

By sending him to be tried before Pilate

MRK 15:1 See also LUK 22:66–23:1

The teachers of the law, with others, mocked Christ crucified

MAT 27:41–27:42 parallel MRK 15:31–15:32

Jesus Christ foretold the opposition of the teachers of the law

MAT 16:21 parallels MRK 8:31, LUK 9:22 See also MAT 20:18–20:19 parallel MRK 10:33–10:34

Teachers of the law in conflict with the apostles

ACT 4:5–4:7, ACT 6:12

Teachers of the law in a non-confrontational setting

MAT 2:4, MAT 8:19–8:20, MAT 13:52; LUK 20:39; 1TI 1:7 See also MAT 7:28–7:29, MAT 17:10 parallel MRK 9:11; MRK 1:22; ACT 23:9

resource.label: 2363: Jesus Christ, preaching and teaching of
8787: opposition, to God's person and work

title: 7466: temples
content: Buildings for the worship of local or national gods. After settling in the promised land, Israel felt the need for a permanent building set apart for worship of the LORD. Three temples were built at different periods in Jerusalem. In the NT the individual believer (or the body of the church) is seen as the true temple or dwelling-place of God.

title: 7467: temple, Solomon's
content:
From the exodus until the reign of Solomon, the tabernacle located in various places served as Israel's “temple” and the ordained place of sacrifice. Solomon built the first temple in Jerusalem.

Centres for worship and sacrifice before the temple was built

JOS 18:1; ; 1SA 7:2

David's preparations for the building and worship in the first temple

The plan for its construction

2SA 7:12–7:13; ; 1CH 17:1 parallel 2SA 7:1; ; 1CH 28:11–28:12, 1CH 28:19

The gifts for its construction

1CH 29:2–29:3, 1CH 29:6

Arrangements for the temple worship

1CH 23:3–23:5

Solomon's construction of the temple

1KI 5:1–5:13, 1KI 6:7; ; 2CH 2:17–2:18

The completion of the temple and its furnishings

2CH 2:5 Although it followed the basic pattern of the tabernacle, Solomon intended the temple to reflect God's greatness. See also 1KI 6:20–6:22 parallel 2CH 3:4–3:9 The inner walls were overlaid with gold; 1KI 6:27 parallel 2CH 3:11–3:12 The wings of the cherubim reached from wall to wall; 1KI 6:381KI 7:23 parallel 2CH 4:2; 1KI 7:26–7:27 parallel 2CH 4:5; 1KI 7:30 1KI 7:49 not one golden lampstand but ten; 2CH 4:8 not one table for the bread of the Presence but ten

The dedication of the temple

At the Feast of Tabernacles

1KI 8:2 parallel 2CH 5:3

The glory of the LORD filled the temple

1KI 8:6 parallel 2CH 5:7; ; 1KI 8:10–8:11; ; 2CH 5:11, 2CH 5:13–5:14

The offering of sacrifices

1KI 8:62–8:63 parallel 2CH 7:5

Solomon's prayer

1KI 8:27 parallel 2CH 6:18

The temple after the division of the kingdom

See also 1KI 12:26–12:30; 2CH 13:4–13:12

Later kings of Judah used the temple treasures for political purposes

1KI 15:18–15:19 parallel 2CH 16:2–16:3 Asa took silver and gold to persuade the king of Aram to be his ally against Israel; ; 2KI 12:17–12:18 Joash sent money and treasures to prevent the king of Aram attacking Jerusalem; ; 2KI 16:7–16:8 parallel 2CH 28:21 Ahaz used gold and silver to gain the support of the king of Assyria against his enemies; ; 2KI 18:14–18:15 Hezekiah used temple silver to placate the king of Assyria.

Foreign kings pillaged the temple

1KI 14:25–14:26 parallel 2CH 12:9 Shishak king of Egypt; ; 2KI 24:13; ; 2CH 36:18 Nebuchadnezzar

Under certain kings the temple was cleansed and repaired

Joash: 2KI 12:4–12:5 parallel 2CH 24:5; ; 2CH 24:13–24:14; 2KI 15:35 parallel 2CH 27:3 Jotham; 2CH 29:3–29:5, 2CH 29:15–29:16, 2CH 29:18–29:19, 2CH 29:25; 2KI 22:3–22:7 parallel 2CH 34:8–34:11 Josiah

The destruction of the temple by the Babylonians

2KI 25:13–25:15 parallel JER 52:17–52:19; ; 2CH 36:18

The temple and the ministry of the prophets

Isaiah received his call in the temple

ISA 6:1–6:8

Ezekiel had a vision of the glory of God departing

EZK 10:18–10:19

Micah's prophecy concerning the temple's fate

JER 26:18; ; MIC 3:12

Jeremiah's ministry was closely linked to the temple

JER 7:2, JER 7:4, JER 7:14, JER 19:14

title: 7468: temple, rebuilding of
content:
Following the exile Solomon's ruined temple was rebuilt and became the focus of the Jewish faith worldwide.

Preparations for rebuilding the temple

EZR 1:1–1:4 parallel 2CH 36:22–36:23 Cyrus gave permission for the exiled Jews to return to Jerusalem to rebuild the temple (538 B.C.); ; EZR 1:7 He sent back the temple treasures.

Rebuilding the temple under the leadership of Jeshua and Zerubbabel

EZR 3:3–3:6, EZR 3:8–3:13, EZR 4:1–4:3

Interruptions to the rebuilding

The Jews' enemies persuaded Artaxerxes to order the work to cease

EZR 4:8, EZR 4:12–4:15, EZR 4:17, EZR 4:20–4:21, EZR 4:24

The prophets Haggai and Zechariah called Zerubbabel and the people to restart work

HAG 1:4 See also EZR 5:1–5:12; HAG 1:5–1:7, HAG 1:14–1:15

The rebuilding authorised by King Darius

EZR 5:3, EZR 5:11, EZR 5:13, EZR 6:3–6:5, EZR 6:7–6:8

The dedication and worship of the rebuilt temple

EZR 6:16, EZR 7:11–7:17

The post-exilic temple and its worship was regarded as the spiritual centre for Jews worldwide

PSA 87:1–87:7, PSA 135:21

The temple is the place where God is to be praised

PSA 100:4, PSA 134:1–134:2 See also PSA 135:19–135:20, PSA 138:2

Prophecies of restoration of the temple

ZEC 1:16 See also JER 31:6, JER 31:23, JER 33:18; EZK 40:1–43:27

title: 7469: temple, Herod's
content:
Herod's temple, familiar to Jesus Christ and his disciples and frequented by the first Christians, was destroyed by the Romans in A.D. 70.

The building of Herod the Great's temple

JHN 2:20

Jesus Christ visited the temple and taught in its courts

He was taken there as a baby

LUK 2:22, LUK 2:34, LUK 2:36–2:38

He stayed in the temple courts after his parents had returned home from the Passover Feast

LUK 2:41–2:43, LUK 2:46

He cast out of the temple court those who used it for material profit

MAT 21:12–21:13 parallels MRK 11:15–11:17, LUK 19:45–19:46; ; JHN 2:14–2:16

He taught in the temple

MRK 12:35, MRK 12:41–12:43 parallel LUK 21:1–21:4; ; JHN 7:14; JHN 7:32, JHN 7:45–7:46; JHN 8:2–8:3, JHN 10:22–10:24

The religious leaders challenged his authority

MAT 21:23 parallels MRK 11:27–11:28, LUK 20:1–20:2

He foretold the complete destruction of the temple buildings

MAT 24:1–24:2 parallels MRK 13:1–13:2, LUK 21:5–21:6

The crucifixion and the temple

One of the charges against Jesus Christ was that he had said he would destroy the temple

MAT 26:60–26:61

The curtain of the temple was torn in two at Jesus Christ's death

MAT 27:51 The curtain symbolised the barrier between God and his people caused by their sins.

Believers in Jerusalem met in the temple courtyards

See also ACT 2:46, ACT 5:12 Solomon's Colonnade was on the east side of the temple courtyard.

Apostles taught and healed in the temple courtyards

ACT 3:1–3:26, ACT 5:19–5:26

Stephen was stoned for allegedly speaking against the temple

ACT 6:12–6:14ACT 7:48–7:49

Paul was accused of taking Gentiles into temple courtyards forbidden to them

See also ACT 21:27–21:28

resource.label: 2363: Jesus Christ, preaching and teaching of

title: 7470: temple, significance of
content:
The temple was both idealised and spiritualised by OT and NT writers.

The OT concept of an ideal temple in the future age of salvation

ISA 2:3 parallel MIC 4:2 See also ISA 56:4–56:7, ISA 66:20; JER 33:11; EZK 37:28, EZK 40:2–40:4 Ezekiel received a vision of a rebuilt temple to which the glory of the LORD had returned. Chapters 40–48 describe this temple and its worship in detail; HAG 2:6–2:8; ZEC 14:16; MAL 3:1–3:3

The superstitious belief that the presence of the temple in Jerusalem safeguarded the city

The origin of such a belief

2KI 19:32–19:36; ; PSA 132:13–132:18

The prophets knew that disobedience to God would be judged in spite of the temple

MIC 3:12 See also JER 7:4, JER 7:12–7:14

The link between the earthly temple and heaven

Heaven is described as God's temple

PSA 11:4 See also HAB 2:20; HEB 8:5, HEB 9:24; REV 11:19, REV 14:15–14:17, REV 15:5–15:8

The ultimate ``city of God'' needs no temple, since it is permeated by God's presence

REV 21:22 See also REV 21:3

The risen Christ as the temple where God's glory is revealed

JHN 2:19–2:21

The church as the temple where God's presence dwells

The individual believer as the temple of the Holy Spirit

1CO 6:19

The local church as God's temple

See also 1CO 3:16–3:17

The whole church as God's temple

EPH 2:21–2:22 See also 2CO 6:16; 1PE 2:5 “house” here alludes to the temple. The church offers not animal sacrifices but “spiritual sacrifices” of praise and service.

title: 7471: temples, heathen
content:
Other nations also built temples to their gods, and apostate kings of Judah and Israel were condemned for erecting heathen temples in their own countries.

Heathen temples

The temple of Dagon: JDG 16:28–16:30; ; 1SA 5:2–5:4; 1SA 31:8–31:10; ; 2KI 5:18 the temple of Rimmon; ; NAM 1:14

Idolatrous temples in Israel and Judah

1KI 12:26–12:30 Jeroboam's temples at Bethel and Dan; 1KI 16:32; ; 2KI 10:21, 2KI 10:23–10:27; 2CH 23:17 A temple to Baal in Judah was destroyed in the reign of Joash.

The command to destroy pagan places of worship in Israel

DEU 12:2–12:3

Holy things and holy places were used for pagan worship

Ahaz: 2KI 16:10–16:13; ; 2CH 28:23; 2KI 21:4–21:5; ; 2CH 33:5, 2CH 33:7; 2CH 24:7 Athaliah's sons used objects from the temple for the worship of the Baals; ; EZK 8:12–8:16 God showed Ezekiel a vision of people worshipping false gods in the temple; ; 2CH 26:16–26:20 Uzziah incurred judgment by usurping the priestly task of offering incense in the temple.

title: 7474: Tent of Meeting
content:
A tent pitched by Moses outside the Israelite camp in the wilderness. There Moses met with God and others would enquire of the LORD. God's presence was shown there by a pillar of cloud. It seems to predate the construction and setting up of the tabernacle, after which, the term became synonymous with the tabernacle.

Moses pitched the Tent of Meeting outside the Israelite camp

EXO 33:7 The tent was outside the camp because of the LORD's estrangement from his people (Ex 33:3) following their making the golden calf. It was possibly a temporary structure used until the tabernacle was completed.

The Tent of Meeting was where the faithful met with God

Moses

See also EXO 33:9, EXO 33:11

Those who wished to enquire of the LORD

EXO 33:7

Joshua

EXO 33:11

The pillar of cloud at the Tent of Meeting

It indicated God's presence

EXO 33:9

Those who saw the cloud worshipped the LORD

EXO 33:10

The Tent of Meeting and the tabernacle

This Tent of Meeting may be distinct from the tabernacle

EXO 25:8–25:9

The tabernacle was constructed (in accordance with detailed instructions) after the Tent of Meeting

See also EXO 35:10–35:11, EXO 39:32–39:43

The tabernacle was set up after the Tent of Meeting

EXO 40:1–40:2, EXO 40:33

The Tent of Meeting as a synonym for the tabernacle

EXO 40:2 Afterwards “Tent of Meeting” became a synonym for the tabernacle, especially the tent part of the whole complex. See also EXO 40:6–40:7, EXO 40:22, EXO 40:24, EXO 40:29–40:30, EXO 40:34–40:35; LEV 1:1, LEV 24:3; NUM 1:1, NUM 31:54; DEU 31:14–31:15; JOS 18:1, JOS 19:51; 1SA 2:22; 1KI 8:4 parallel 2CH 5:5; 1CH 6:32, 1CH 9:21; 2CH 1:3

title: 7476: thank-offering
content:
Together with vows and freewill offerings this was one of the three kinds of fellowship offering.

Presenting a thank-offering

An animal sacrifice

LEV 7:15 See also LEV 22:29

Accompanied by bread, wafers and cakes

LEV 7:12–7:13 Cakes made with yeast can be offered, since these are not to be burned on the altar.

The priest shares the meal

LEV 7:14, LEV 22:29–22:30

The occasions for presenting thank-offerings

For deliverance from distress, death or sickness

PSA 50:23, PSA 107:21–107:22 See also PSA 56:12–56:13 deliverance from death; PSA 116:17 Deliverance from sickness; the earlier verses show the psalmist has been ill.

Following a vow

PSA 7:17 A vow to praise God in anticipation of an answer to prayer. See also PSA 66:13–66:14

At times of religious renewal

2CH 29:31 Hezekiah's reformation; ; 2CH 33:15–33:16 Manasseh's repentance; JER 17:26, JER 33:11

title: 7478: washing
content:
The physical or ritual cleansing of part or all of a person's body, a person's clothing or certain vessels. Washing can be symbolic of purification from defilement or cleansing from sin. It can also be part of preparation for a special act of religious service.

Washing as the cleansing of the body, clothing or certain vessels

EXO 30:17–30:21; LEV 13:53–13:54 See also EXO 40:30–40:32; LEV 6:27, LEV 14:8–14:9, LEV 15:2–15:13, LEV 16:24, LEV 16:26–16:28; NUM 31:24; 2CH 4:6 parallel 1KI 7:38; JHN 2:6

Jesus Christ taught that inner purity was more important than ritual washing

MAT 15:17–15:20 parallel MRK 7:20–7:23 See also MRK 7:1–7:9 parallel MAT 15:1–15:3; MRK 7:14–7:15 parallel MAT 15:10–15:11; LUK 11:37–11:41

Washing as a symbolic act

Of purification from defilement

LEV 11:24–11:25 See also LEV 11:28, LEV 11:40, LEV 13:6, LEV 13:34, LEV 15:4–15:8, LEV 17:15–17:16; DEU 23:10–23:11; 2KI 5:10

Of cleansing from sin

1CO 6:9–6:11; EPH 5:25–5:27 See also PSA 51:2, PSA 51:7; ISA 1:16, ISA 4:4; JER 4:14; ACT 22:16; TIT 3:5; HEB 10:22; JAS 4:8; REV 7:14, REV 22:14

Of personal cleansing before a special act of religious service

LEV 16:24 See also EXO 19:10–19:11, EXO 29:4, EXO 40:12–40:13, EXO 40:30–40:32; LEV 8:6; NUM 8:6–8:7, NUM 8:21

Washing one's hands was used as a symbolic declaration of innocence

DEU 21:6–21:7 See also PSA 26:6, PSA 73:13; MAT 27:24

title: 7480: wave offering
content:
A gift specially dedicated to God. The term itself may not be intended to convey a particular action.

The wave offering was part of every fellowship offering

LEV 7:34 Although given to the priests, the offering belonged to the LORD. See also LEV 7:28–7:32, LEV 9:21

The wave offering was eaten by the priests

EXO 29:26–29:28; ; LEV 10:15; ; NUM 18:11, NUM 18:18

Occasions for making a wave offering

As a cereal offering when priests were consecrated

EXO 29:23–29:24; ; LEV 8:27–8:29

As a guilt offering sacrificed at a ceremonial cleansing

LEV 14:12, LEV 14:21, LEV 14:24

As part of the ritual when a woman was accused of adultery

NUM 5:25

When celebrating harvest

LEV 23:10–23:11 the firstfruits of the harvest; LEV 23:15–23:17, LEV 23:20

When presenting a gift to God

Materials given for constructing the tabernacle: EXO 35:22, EXO 38:24, EXO 38:29

When dedicating the Levites to God

NUM 8:11 The dedication to God of the Levites symbolised the consecration of the whole nation. See also NUM 8:13, NUM 8:15, NUM 8:21

title: 7482: Year of Jubilee
content:
Celebrated in the fiftieth year as the culmination of seven sabbatical years. During this year property was restored and Hebrew slaves were released. This year of liberation gave opportunity for a new start, and is alluded to as a type of the Messianic age.

The institution of the Year of Jubilee

LEV 25:8–25:10

Land was left fallow in the Year of Jubilee

See also LEV 25:11–25:12, LEV 25:20–25:22 These instructions apply to the sabbatical year. The Year of Jubilee may have been an additional fallow year, or may have coincided with the seventh sabbatical year.

The restoration of property in the Year of Jubilee

LEV 25:13

Regulating the value of property

See also LEV 25:15–25:16, LEV 27:16–27:19, LEV 27:23

Underlining the fact that God is the true owner of the land

LEV 25:23–25:24 The restoration of property to those to whom God had originally entrusted it is a reminder that the land belongs to him. See also JOS 21:43; 1CH 29:15; HEB 11:13

A means of preserving the inheritance from God

LEV 25:25–25:28 Families are urged to buy back land assigned to them by God. As a last resort, land is restored in the Year of Jubilee. See also LEV 25:32–25:33, LEV 27:24; NUM 36:4, NUM 36:7–36:9; 1KI 21:3; EZK 46:16–46:18

The release of Hebrew slaves in the Year of Jubilee

DEU 15:12–15:15 The release of slaves reflects God's redemption and preserves the freedom he has given; LEV 25:54–25:55 The Israelites may not be held permanently as slaves because they belong to God. See also LEV 25:39–25:43, LEV 25:50–25:52; JER 34:8–34:9, JER 34:13–34:14

The Year of Jubilee is the year of the Lord's favour

LUK 4:18–4:19 Jesus Christ alludes to the Year of Jubilee in proclaiming spiritual release and restoration. See also ISA 61:1–61:2

title: 7500: Jews and Gentiles
content:
• 7505: Jews, the
  • 7510: Gentiles
  • 7515: anti-semitism
  • 7520: dispersion, the
  • 7525: exclusiveness
  • 7530: foreigners
  • 7535: Greeks
  • 7540: Judaism
  • 7545: outsiders
  • 7550: Pharisees
  • 7555: Sadducees
  • 7560: Samaritans, the
  • 7565: Sanhedrin
  • 7570: sects

title: 7505: Jews, the
content:
A post-exilic designation for descendants of the people from the kingdom of Judah and also for adherents to their religion from other nations. The term is used extensively in the NT where it has a variety of nuances.

Early use of the term ``Jews''

Pre-exilic and exilic use

JER 40:11–40:12 See also JER 32:12, JER 34:8–34:9, JER 38:19, JER 40:15, JER 41:3, JER 43:8–43:9, JER 44:1, JER 44:26–44:27, JER 52:28–52:30; DAN 3:8–3:12

Used of the citizens of the province of Judah

EZR 5:1 See also EZR 4:23, EZR 5:5, EZR 6:7–6:8, EZR 6:14; NEH 2:16, NEH 4:1–4:2, NEH 4:12, NEH 5:6–5:8, NEH 5:17, NEH 6:5–6:7; ZEC 8:23

Used of the descendants of the exiles from the southern kingdom

EST 2:5 See also EZR 4:11–4:12, EZR 7:12–7:20; EST 3:1–3:6, EST 3:13, EST 4:3, EST 4:12–4:14, EST 6:12–6:13, EST 8:5–8:13, EST 9:1–9:3, EST 9:30–9:31, EST 10:3

NT use of the term ``Jews''

Jewish people in general

MRK 7:3 See also MAT 28:15; LUK 7:3; JHN 2:6, JHN 4:9, JHN 4:19–4:22, JHN 8:31–8:32, JHN 11:18–11:19, JHN 11:31–11:33, JHN 11:45, JHN 12:9–12:11, JHN 18:20

Those Jewish leaders who were hostile to Jesus Christ

JHN 19:31 See also JHN 2:18–2:20, JHN 5:1–5:18, JHN 6:35–6:42, JHN 7:1, JHN 7:11–7:15, JHN 8:48–8:59, JHN 9:1–9:34, JHN 10:22–10:33, JHN 18:12–18:14, JHN 18:28–18:31, JHN 19:38, JHN 20:19

Jesus Christ as ``king of the Jews''

MAT 27:37 parallels MRK 15:26, LUK 23:38, JHN 19:19–19:21 See also MAT 2:1–2:2, MAT 27:11 parallels MRK 15:2, JHN 18:33; MAT 27:27–27:31 parallel MRK 15:16–15:20; JHN 19:1–19:6; MRK 15:6–15:14 parallel JHN 18:38–18:40

The gospel first preached to the Jews

ACT 11:19; ROM 10:16–10:21 See also ACT 2:1–2:41, ACT 10:23–10:48, ACT 13:14–13:43; ROM 1:16, ROM 15:8–15:9; GAL 2:7–2:9

Opposition of certain Jews to the gospel

ACT 20:19 See also ACT 6:8–6:14, ACT 9:23–9:25, ACT 9:28–9:30, ACT 12:1–12:3, ACT 13:44–13:45, ACT 13:49–13:50, ACT 14:1–14:6, ACT 14:19, ACT 17:4–17:9, ACT 17:13, ACT 18:1–18:6, ACT 18:12–18:17, ACT 20:3; 2CO 11:23–11:25; ACT 21:27–21:36, ACT 23:12–23:15, ACT 24:1–24:9, ACT 25:1–25:7, ACT 26:19–26:21

Believers described as Jews and Gentiles

ACT 6:1 See also ACT 21:20–21:21, ACT 21:39, ACT 22:1–22:3; ROM 9:23–9:24; 1CO 1:22–1:24, 1CO 12:13

Jews and Gentiles equal in Christ

ROM 11:25–11:32; GAL 3:26–3:29 See also ROM 3:27–3:30, ROM 10:12–10:13; 1CO 12:12–12:13; COL 3:11

Jews and Gentiles equally in need of salvation

ROM 3:1–3:20

resource.label: 7135: Israel, as the people of God
8787: opposition, to God's person and work

title: 7510: Gentiles
content: Those who are not physical descendants of Abraham and were therefore regarded as being excluded from the promise made to him and his descendants. The Gentiles are often referred to as “the nations” in OT writings.

title: 7511: Gentiles, in OT
content:
Under the OT, Gentiles were generally excluded from the worship of the synagogue and from all the privileges of being Jewish. However, the OT made provision for non-Jews who believed in the God of Israel.

Believing foreigners were permitted to participate in worship

EXO 12:48–12:49 See also NUM 9:14, NUM 15:13–15:16; 1KI 8:41–8:43; ISA 56:6–56:8

Foreigners living in Israel were to be treated kindly

EXO 23:9 See also EXO 22:21; LEV 19:9–19:10, LEV 24:22; DEU 26:12–26:15, DEU 27:19

Divine promises concerning the Gentiles

All people will be blessed through Abraham

GEN 12:3 See also GEN 18:18, GEN 22:18; GAL 3:8–3:9

All nations will bow before God

PSA 22:27–22:28 See also PSA 86:9; DAN 7:14; AMO 9:11–9:12

The Gentiles will be enlightened

ISA 49:6 See also ISA 9:1–9:2, ISA 42:6, ISA 60:1–60:3; LUK 2:30–2:32

The Messiah will bring justice to the Gentiles

MAT 12:18–12:21 See also ISA 42:1–42:4

Gentiles will become God's people

ROM 9:22–9:25 See also HOS 2:23; 1PE 2:10

Gentiles will praise God

ROM 15:9–15:12 See also DEU 32:43; 2SA 22:50; PSA 18:49; ISA 11:10

title: 7512: Gentiles, in NT
content:
Although Jesus Christ initially preached to Israel, his mission was soon extended to the Gentiles. From the outset, the Christian church accepted Gentiles as full members, refusing to make any distinction between Jew and Gentile. Paul, “the apostle to the Gentiles”, laid particular emphasis on their role in the purposes of God.

Gentiles in Jesus Christ's earthly ministry

Jesus Christ's contact with Gentiles

MAT 8:5–8:13 parallel LUK 7:1–7:10; ; MAT 15:21–15:28 parallel MRK 7:24–7:30

Jesus Christ's teaching concerning Gentiles

MAT 21:33–21:43 parallels MRK 12:1–12:11, LUK 20:9–20:16LUK 21:20–21:24

Jesus Christ's instructions to his disciples concerning Gentiles

See also MAT 10:5–10:6 Initially the disciples were commanded to preach to Jews, but after his resurrection, Jesus Christ commissioned them to preach the message of the kingdom of God to all nations.MAT 28:19–28:20MRK 16:15–16:16; LUK 24:46–24:47

Peter sent to Gentiles

ACT 10:28 See also ACT 10:9–10:20, ACT 11:5–11:14ACT 10:44–10:48 See also ACT 11:15, ACT 15:7–15:8

Paul, apostle to the Gentiles

GAL 2:7–2:9 See also ACT 9:15, ACT 22:21, ACT 26:15–26:23; ROM 1:13–1:15, ROM 11:13, ROM 15:15–15:19; GAL 2:2; EPH 3:1; 1TI 2:7; 2TI 4:17

Rejected by Jews, Paul turns more to the Gentiles

ACT 18:6 See also ACT 13:46, ACT 28:28

Response of the Gentiles to Paul's preaching

ACT 13:48 See also ACT 14:1–14:2, ACT 15:12, ACT 21:19; 1CO 1:23–1:24

Difficulties concerning the acceptance of Gentiles into the church

The question of mixing with Gentiles

ACT 11:2–11:3 See also ACT 11:15–11:18; GAL 2:12–2:13

The question of circumcision

ACT 15:5–15:31 See also GAL 2:1–2:5, GAL 5:1–5:6

NT teaching on Gentiles

Gentiles now share the inheritance of God's people Israel

EPH 2:13; REV 5:9–5:10 See also ROM 3:29, ROM 11:11–11:21; GAL 3:29; REV 7:9, REV 14:6

There is no distinction between Jews and Gentiles

GAL 3:28 See also ROM 2:9–2:11; EPH 2:19–2:20; COL 3:11

God calls both Jew and Gentile

JHN 10:16; ACT 13:47–13:48 See also ROM 9:24–9:26; 1CO 1:24–1:25; 2TI 4:17

Both Jews and Gentiles are justified by faith

GAL 3:6–3:14 See also ROM 4:9–4:12, ROM 4:16; GAL 5:6

title: 7515: anti-semitism
content:
A modern term, used to refer to a hatred of Jews or Arabs. Traces of anti-semitism may be discerned in biblical times.

The development of anti-semitism

EXO 1:8–1:14 Strictly speaking, this hostility towards the Israelites in Egypt after the death of Joseph was not anti-semitism as this word is now understood. See also EXO 1:15–1:16, EXO 1:22, EXO 3:9, EXO 5:6–5:9

Anti-semitism under Persian rule

EST 3:1–3:14 See also EZR 4:1–4:8, EZR 4:24

Anti-semitism under Babylonian rule

DAN 3:8–3:23 See also DAN 6:3–6:14

Anti-semitism under Roman rule

ACT 18:1–18:2

title: 7520: dispersion, the
content:
A technical term used to refer to God's people as they are scattered throughout the world.

The dispersion of the Jews

Promised and predicted

DEU 4:25–4:28; 1KI 14:15 See also DEU 28:15–28:68; LEV 26:27–26:35; NEH 1:8–1:9; JER 13:24; EZK 6:8–6:10, EZK 20:23–20:24, EZK 22:15

The prediction fulfilled

JER 50:17; DAN 9:7 See also EST 3:8; PSA 44:9–44:16; LAM 4:16; EZK 36:17–36:20; ZEP 3:10; ZEC 1:19; JHN 7:35 In NT times there were more Jews living outside Palestine than within.

The promise of return

ISA 11:12 See also DEU 4:29–4:31; JER 31:10; EZK 11:16–11:17

The dispersion of Christians

ACT 8:1 Christians scattered because of persecution; 1PE 1:1 See also ACT 8:4, ACT 11:19; JAS 1:1

title: 7525: exclusiveness
content:
The keeping of a distinctive group identity by drawing clear boundary lines, to separate those within the group from outsiders with whom they are unwilling to associate.

The practice of exclusiveness by God's people, grounded in God's claim to exclusive worship

EXO 20:3 parallel DEU 5:7 See also EXO 34:14; DEU 6:14–6:15; 2KI 17:35–17:39; ISA 42:8

Exclusiveness in Israel, a practical expression of being a people set apart

An expression of being set apart for God

LEV 20:26 See also LEV 15:31, LEV 20:23–20:24; DEU 7:1–7:6

An expression of being set apart from others

NUM 23:9 See also JOS 23:7; EZR 6:21; NEH 13:1–13:3; EZK 44:9

The practice of exclusiveness

Expressed in the avoidance of marrying outsiders

NEH 10:30 See also DEU 7:3–7:4; EZR 9:1–9:4, EZR 10:9–10:17; NEH 13:23–13:27

Expressed in a refusal to eat with outsiders

MAT 9:10–9:11 parallels MRK 2:15–2:16, LUK 5:29–5:30 See also LUK 15:1–15:2; GAL 2:12

The marks of Jewish exclusiveness

Circumcision

GEN 17:9–17:14 See also EXO 12:48; LEV 12:3; ACT 7:8

Festival observance

EXO 12:14–12:16 See also EXO 12:17–12:20; LEV 23:26–23:29; NUM 9:13

Observance of food laws

LEV 3:17 See also LEV 7:22–7:27, LEV 11:1–11:47, LEV 17:10–17:14; ACT 15:29 While food restrictions were not a requirement of the gospel, Gentile believers are here asked to respect the scruples of Jewish Christians in those areas that were traditionally important to them.

Sexual relations restricted

LEV 18:6 See also LEV 18:7–18:30, LEV 20:10–20:21; DEU 27:20–27:23

Effects of Jewish exclusiveness

Division between Jews and Samaritans

JHN 4:9 See also EZR 4:1–4:3; MAT 10:5–10:6 Jesus Christ was here dealing with his priority for that time; LUK 9:51–9:53

Division between Jews and Gentiles

ACT 10:28 See also MAT 10:5, MAT 15:21–15:28 parallel MRK 7:24–7:30 Jesus Christ restricted his earthly ministry in general to the people of Israel, but instigated a mission to the Gentiles after his resurrection; JHN 18:28

The problem of Jewish exclusiveness among the first Christians

The negative evaluation of Gentiles

ACT 11:1–11:18 See also GAL 2:11–2:13, GAL 2:15

The requirement that Gentile believers be circumcised

ACT 15:1 See also ACT 15:5; GAL 5:2–5:6, GAL 6:12; TIT 1:10

Jewish exclusiveness overturned

By God giving his Spirit to the uncircumcised Gentiles

ACT 15:8 See also ACT 10:44–11:18; 1CO 12:13; GAL 3:2–3:5

By the argument of justification by faith

GAL 2:15–2:16 See also ACT 15:9–15:11; ROM 3:28–3:30, ROM 4:9–4:12

resource.label: 8269: holiness, as separation from the worldly

title: 7530: foreigners
content:
Foreigners played a major role in the life of Israel. They surrounded her, and lived within her borders. In order to fulfil her mission as the bearer of God's law, Israel had to distinguish herself from foreign nations and individuals, especially with regard to their religious beliefs. A clear distinction is made between foreign nations and foreign individuals, who are to be allowed to live freely among Israelites within the land.

The origins of foreign nations

GEN 9:19 See also GEN 11:1–11:9

The foreign nations of Ammon and Moab

GEN 19:30–19:38

The relation of Israel and Edom

GEN 25:21–25:34

Israel's relationship with foreign nations

Israel's captivity in Egypt

EXO 6:6 See also AMO 1:3, AMO 1:6, AMO 1:9, AMO 1:11, AMO 1:13; ISA 10:24–10:25 God's judgment on Assyria; ISA 47:6 the fall of Babylon prophesied; EZK 25:3, EZK 25:8, EZK 25:12, EZK 25:15, EZK 26:2; NAM 1:14 Nineveh's destruction foretold

Foreign nations as a threat to Israel

DEU 7:1–7:4 See also GEN 9:25, GEN 15:16 “Amorites” and “Canaanites” are both terms used to describe generically the races expelled by Israel from the land of promise, a fate brought on themselves by flagrant sin; LEV 18:25, LEV 20:23; DEU 18:12

The need for Israel to keep separate from foreign nations

LEV 20:26 See also NUM 23:9; DEU 7:4; JOS 23:7; JDG 2:2; 1KI 11:1–11:2, 1KI 16:31; EZR 4:3, EZR 6:21, EZR 9:12, EZR 10:11; NEH 10:30, NEH 13:3 Foreign religious beliefs and practices were seen as a constant threat to faith in the LORD. The command that Israel keep apart from foreign nations partly reflects a concern that Israel might adopt religious beliefs which compromise her commitment to the LORD.

Foreign nations are described as uncircumcised

ISA 52:1 Circumcision was a sign of being within God's covenant. But some foreigners were also circumcised (Jer 9:25–26). See also GEN 34:14; EXO 12:48; JDG 14:3, JDG 15:18; 1SA 14:6, 1SA 17:26; 2SA 1:20; EZK 28:10, EZK 32:26

Israel's relation with foreigners within her boundaries

Foreigners are to be treated graciously

LEV 19:34 See also LEV 19:10, LEV 23:22; DEU 10:19, DEU 14:29, DEU 24:19–24:21, DEU 26:12–26:13

Foreigners must not be oppressed

LEV 19:33 See also EXO 22:21, EXO 23:9; DEU 24:14, DEU 24:17, DEU 27:19; PSA 146:9; JER 7:6, JER 22:3; ZEC 7:10

Foreigners have a positive role to play in Israel

ISA 14:1

The rights of foreigners within Israel

Foreigners are to share in Sabbath-rest

See also EXO 20:10 parallel DEU 5:14; EXO 23:12

Foreigners are to receive a fair trial

DEU 1:16

Foreigners are to participate in festivals and ceremonies

DEU 16:11, DEU 16:14, DEU 26:11, DEU 29:10–29:11, DEU 31:12; ; 2CH 30:25

Ultimately inheritance rights were envisaged for foreigners

EZK 47:22–47:23

The obligations of foreigners are similar to those of native Israelites

NUM 9:14 See also EXO 12:19, EXO 12:48–12:49; LEV 16:29, LEV 17:8, LEV 17:12–17:13, LEV 17:15, LEV 18:26, LEV 20:2, LEV 22:18, LEV 24:16 The penalties for blasphemy apply to Israelite and foreigner alike; LEV 24:22; NUM 15:14–15:16 The rules on the presentation of offerings are to be the same for both the Israelites and the foreigner; NUM 19:10, NUM 35:15; EZK 14:7

Occasional distinctions made for foreigners

LEV 25:47–25:48; ; DEU 14:21, DEU 28:43

title: 7535: Greeks
content:
The inhabitants of Greece and her empire. The term is also used to describe non-Jews, so that “Greek”, in the NT, is virtually synonymous with “Gentile”. While distinctions between Greek and Jew on the one hand, and Greek and barbarian on the other, were important in the world at large, such divisions are to have no place within the church.

The cultural distinctiveness of Greeks in NT times

JHN 7:35 See also MRK 7:26; ACT 6:1, ACT 9:29; ROM 1:14

``Greeks'' used to describe non-Jews

ACT 21:27–21:28 See also ACT 16:1–16:3, ACT 20:21; GAL 2:3

``Greeks'' used to refer to God-fearers

JHN 12:20 See also ACT 17:4, ACT 17:12, ACT 17:17, ACT 18:4

Greeks may reject the gospel as foolishness

1CO 1:22–1:23 The high value placed by the Greeks upon human wisdom could hinder their response to the gospel. See also ACT 17:18–17:34

In Christ there is no distinction between Greeks and Jews

Greeks and Jews share the same salvation

ROM 10:11–10:13 See also ROM 3:28–3:30; 1CO 1:22–1:24

Greeks and Jews are united in Christ

COL 3:11 See also 1CO 12:13; GAL 3:26–3:29

title: 7540: Judaism
content:
Derived from “Judah” (the southern kingdom of the divided monarchy) the term refers to the religion and culture of the Jews from the time of the exile (586 B.C.) onwards.

The exile and the beginnings of Judaism

The people of Judah become known as Jews

EZR 4:12; ; NEH 4:2; ; EST 3:6; ; JER 32:12; ; MAT 2:2; ; JHN 2:6

Jewish communities were formed in many places

JER 44:1; ; EZR 2:1 many stayed in Babylon; ; JHN 7:35; ; ACT 2:8–2:11

Far from the Jerusalem temple, the exiles had difficulty in knowing how to worship

PSA 137:4 See also HOS 3:4 This prophecy to Israel also expresses the dilemma of the Jerusalem exiles.

New forms of religion after the exile, probably beginning in Babylon

Emphasis on the Scriptures

NEH 7:73–8:3

Synagogue worship

LUK 4:16–4:17 Unable to sacrifice in the temple, the exiles would have focused their worship on the Scriptures, a practice which developed into synagogue worship.

Carefully observing the law

Ezra went from Babylon to Jerusalem with the express intention of seeing that God's law was observed: EZR 7:11, EZR 7:14, EZR 7:25

The temple and sacrifice

The returning exiles recommence the sacrifices

EZR 3:1–3:6

The temple rebuilt

EZR 3:8, EZR 6:14–6:15

The temple as a focus for pilgrims

JHN 12:20; ; ACT 2:5 They had probably come for Passover and were staying until Pentecost; ; ACT 8:27

The temple regarded with great reverence

MAT 26:59–26:61 parallel MRK 14:57–14:58; ; ACT 6:13, ACT 21:27–21:29

Studying and obeying the law

Studying Scripture

JHN 5:39

Total obedience essential

MAT 23:23; ; JAS 2:10

Human rules added to God's law

MAT 23:16–23:18; ; MRK 7:1–7:13 parallel MAT 15:1–15:9

High regard for the Sabbath and traditional rules associated with it

MAT 12:1–12:2 parallels MRK 2:23–2:24, LUK 6:1–6:2

Groups within NT Judaism

Pharisees

MRK 7:3–7:4 The Pharisees stressed the fulfilment of the law by individuals. See also LUK 11:38, LUK 18:10–18:12; ACT 26:5

Sadducees

ACT 5:17 A powerful minority group, politically astute whilst theologically conservative, they saw temple worship as the chief purpose of the law. See also MAT 3:7; MRK 12:18 parallels MAT 22:23, LUK 20:27

Teachers of the law (scribes)

They preserved, copied, taught and administered the law: MAT 17:10; ; MRK 9:14; ; LUK 2:46; ; ACT 4:5

Zealots

LUK 6:15 They favoured a “holy war” to drive out the Romans.

Non-Jewish converts

MAT 23:15; ; ACT 2:11, ACT 6:5, ACT 13:43

Judaism and Christianity

Conflict between Jesus Christ and the Jewish authorities

MRK 14:1 See also JHN 11:45–11:50

Conflict between the first Christians and Judaism

JHN 16:2; ; ACT 4:1–4:3, ACT 5:17–5:18; ; GAL 1:13–1:14

Conflict produced by Jewish influence among the first Christians

ACT 15:5 See also ACT 15:1–15:2; GAL 2:11–2:16 Judaism gave birth to Christianity. After an initial uneasy co-existence a total break finally ensued.

title: 7545: outsiders
content:
Israel recognised two general categories of outsiders: those who lived outside Israel, usually referred to as “the nations” or “the Gentiles”, and foreigners resident within Israel's boundaries. The latter, usually referred to as “aliens”, were given considerable privileges under the covenant between God and Israel.

Israel's attitude to the Gentiles as outsiders

The nations as outsiders

LEV 20:26; ; NEH 1:8; ; PSA 2:1, PSA 46:10, PSA 106:35; ; HOS 7:8; ; JOL 2:17

The conversion of the Gentiles foretold

ISA 60:3 See also GEN 22:18; PSA 22:27; ISA 9:2; HOS 2:23

Israel's mission to the Gentiles was fulfilled in Jesus Christ

ACT 13:47 See also ISA 49:6; LUK 2:32; ACT 28:28; ROM 11:11; EPH 3:6

Jesus Christ calls both Jew and Gentile

JHN 10:16 See also ACT 13:47–13:48; ROM 9:24–9:26; 1CO 1:24; 2TI 4:17

Israel's attitude to aliens as outsiders

Aliens are to be treated as members of the people of God

EXO 23:9 See also DEU 29:9–29:15, DEU 31:12; JOS 8:33–8:35

Aliens are protected by God

PSA 146:9

Aliens must not be oppressed

JER 22:3 See also JER 7:2–7:7; EZK 22:29; ZEC 7:9–7:10

Aliens may share in Israel's worship

EXO 12:17–12:20, EXO 12:43–12:49; ; LEV 22:17–22:19; ; DEU 16:13–16:15

The Samaritans as outsiders in Israel in NT times

JHN 4:9 By including Samaritans in his ministry, Jesus Christ demonstrated his concern for those who were treated as outsiders by society. See also LUK 10:33, LUK 17:16; JHN 4:7

Christians are compared to aliens in the world

1PE 2:11

The witness of believers to outsiders

COL 4:5 See also MRK 4:11; 1CO 5:12–5:13; 1TH 4:11–4:12; 1TI 3:7

title: 7550: Pharisees
content: A Jewish religious party whose members required a very strict adherence to the ritual law and to the traditions of their predecessors. They were hostile to the teaching of Jesus Christ, which they regarded as compromising their interpretation of the law.

title: 7551: Pharisees, beliefs of
content:
The Pharisees placed considerable emphasis upon the rigorous upholding of the law, particularly the traditional forms in which the law had been established by their predecessors.

Pharisaic interpretation of the law

Pharisees were the recognised custodians of the law

MAT 23:1–23:3

Pharisees emphasised the traditions of the elders

MRK 7:3–7:4 For the Pharisees the oral law, the traditions of their predecessors, had an authority equal to that of the written law. See also MAT 15:1–15:2 parallel MRK 7:5; GAL 1:14

Pharisaic observance of the law

The Sabbath

MAT 12:1–12:2 parallels MRK 2:23–2:24, LUK 6:1–6:2 See also LUK 13:14

Fasting

MAT 9:14 parallels MRK 2:18, LUK 5:33 See also LUK 18:9–18:12

Tithing

MAT 23:23 parallel LUK 11:42

Pharisaic doctrine

ACT 23:6–23:9

Pharisaic proselytism

MAT 23:15

title: 7552: Pharisees, attitudes to Jesus Christ
content:
Pharisees opposed Jesus Christ during his ministry and were among those who brought about his crucifixion. Some Pharisees, however, accepted Christ's teaching and believed in him.

Dialogue between Jesus Christ and Pharisees

They demanded a sign to prove his messiahship

MAT 12:38–12:42 parallel LUK 11:29–11:32 See also MAT 16:1–16:4 parallel MRK 8:11–8:12

They questioned him on matters of the law

MAT 19:3–19:9 parallel MRK 10:2–10:9 See also MAT 22:15–22:22 parallels MRK 12:13–12:17, LUK 20:20–20:26; MAT 22:34–22:40 parallel MRK 12:28–12:34; JHN 8:3–8:11

They called him Teacher

MRK 12:13–12:14 parallel MAT 22:15–22:16 See also MAT 12:38, MAT 22:34–22:36; LUK 7:39–7:40, LUK 19:39, LUK 20:21, LUK 20:28; JHN 3:2, JHN 8:3–8:4

Jesus Christ questioned them

MAT 22:41–22:46 parallels MRK 12:35–12:40, LUK 20:41–20:44 See also MAT 12:9–12:14 parallels MRK 3:1–3:6, LUK 6:6–6:11; MAT 21:24–21:27 parallels MRK 11:29–11:33, LUK 20:3–20:8; LUK 14:1–14:6

Social contact between Jesus Christ and Pharisees

LUK 7:36 See also MRK 7:1–7:2; LUK 11:37, LUK 13:31, LUK 14:1

Jesus Christ criticised the Pharisees

LUK 12:1 See also MAT 5:20, MAT 16:5–16:12 parallel MRK 8:14–8:21; MAT 21:33–21:45 parallels MRK 12:1–12:11, LUK 20:9–20:19; MAT 23:1–23:36 parallels MRK 12:38–12:40, LUK 20:45–20:47; LUK 11:37–11:52, LUK 16:1–16:15, LUK 18:9–18:14

The Pharisees opposed Jesus Christ

LUK 11:53–11:54 MAT 9:2–9:7 parallels MRK 2:3–2:12, LUK 5:17–5:26; MAT 9:10–9:13 parallels MRK 2:15–2:17, LUK 5:29–5:32; MAT 9:32–9:34, MAT 12:22–12:24; ; MRK 3:22; MAT 12:1–12:2 parallels MRK 2:23–2:24, LUK 6:1–6:2; MAT 12:9–12:14; ; MRK 3:1–3:6; ; LUK 6:7, LUK 14:1–14:6; ; JHN 9:13–9:16; LUK 7:36–7:39, LUK 15:1–15:2, LUK 19:37–19:40; ; JHN 8:13, JHN 9:39–9:41

The Pharisees were among those who engineered Jesus Christ's death

MRK 3:6 parallels MAT 12:14, LUK 6:11 See also MAT 21:45–21:46 parallels MRK 12:12, LUK 20:19; MRK 12:13 parallel LUK 20:20; MAT 27:62–27:64; JHN 7:32–7:49

Some Pharisees believed in Jesus Christ

PHP 3:4–3:11 See also JHN 3:1–3:2, JHN 12:42, JHN 19:38–19:40; ACT 15:5, ACT 23:6–23:9, ACT 26:5

title: 7555: Sadducees
content:
An influential Jewish party that denied the existence of the spirit world and the possibility of resurrection. Its members are portrayed as opposing Jesus Christ and the first Christians.

Many priests were Sadducees

ACT 5:17

Sadducees rejected the concepts of resurrection and spirit world

ACT 23:8

Sadducees questioned Jesus Christ about the resurrection

MAT 22:23–22:32 parallels MRK 12:18–12:27, LUK 20:27–20:38

Sadducees denounced by John the Baptist

MAT 3:7–3:10

Sadducees denounced by Jesus Christ

MAT 16:6–16:12 See also MAT 21:28–21:45 The chief priests were probably Sadducees (see Ac 5:17).

The Sadducees oppose the first Christians

ACT 4:1–4:3 See also ACT 5:17–5:18

resource.label: 2363: Jesus Christ, preaching and teaching of
8787: opposition, to God's person and work

title: 7560: Samaritans, the
content:
The people of the northern kingdom of Israel, named after its capital city established by Omri. At the time of the NT, Samaritans were despised by Jews, on account of their intermarriage with Gentiles after the fall of the northern kingdom in 721 B.C. However, the NT presents them as generally responding favourably to the gospel.

Samaria as the name of the northern kingdom

1KI 21:1 The entire northern kingdom is represented by the name of its royal capital, just as Jerusalem often represents Judah. See also 1KI 18:1–18:6; 2KI 17:24, 2KI 23:19; JER 31:5

The fall of Samaria

2KI 17:3–17:5

Samaria's population deported

2KI 17:6–17:18 Sargon II of Assyria completed the siege begun by Shalmaneser V, deporting (according to his own annals) over 27,000 of Samaria's inhabitants. See also 2KI 18:11–18:12

Samaria resettled by other peoples

2KI 17:24 This resettlement, the first of several, led to intermarriage, and is seen by many as the origin of the Samaritans of NT times. See also EZR 4:2, EZR 4:9–4:10

Religion in Samaria after its fall

Syncretism arises in Samaria

2KI 17:25–17:41 Those resettled brought their own gods with them, but also worshipped the LORD as the god of the land. Ultimately these inter-mixed peoples would abandon their polytheism and would accept the law of Moses.

Some in Samaria remain faithful and still make pilgrimage to Jerusalem

2CH 30:10–30:11 See also 2CH 30:1, 2CH 34:9; JER 41:4–41:5

Samaritans and the restoration of Jerusalem

Samaritans offer to rebuild the temple with the returning exiles but are rejected

EZR 4:1–4:5 The response of the Samaritans to this rejection, based on the Jews' desire to keep the faith pure, reflects the double-mindedness of their offer.

Ongoing opposition from the Samaritans to the work of the returned exiles

See also EZR 4:6, EZR 4:7–4:23

Samaria's religious background by the time of the NT

Samaria had established its own temple

JHN 4:20 The Samaritans built their own temple on Mount Gerizim. It was later destroyed by the Jews, which led to a hardening of attitudes between the two groups.

Samaritan Scriptures contained only the Pentateuch

JHN 4:22 Jesus Christ highlights the limited revelation of the Samaritans. Since their Scriptures contained only the Pentateuch, they were expecting a Messiah that they could know little about.

Samaritan and Jewish attitudes to one another

JHN 4:9 Samaritans were seen as being very lax in their religious observance, and Jews would not therefore share drinking vessels with them. By NT times the gulf between Jew and Samaritan was quite wide and bitter. See also LUK 9:51–9:56 The three-day journey from Galilee to Jerusalem involved overnight accommodation, which the Samaritans generally refused, causing many Jews to travel on the eastern side of the Jordan.

Samaritans and the ministry of Jesus Christ

Initially Jesus Christ instructs his disciples not to go to the Samaritans

MAT 10:5–10:6

Jesus Christ is opposed by some Samaritans

See also LUK 9:51–9:56

Jesus Christ ministers to a Samaritan woman and many Samaritans believe in him

JHN 4:4–4:30, JHN 4:39–4:42

Jesus Christ heals a Samaritan leper

LUK 17:11–17:19

Jesus Christ tells a parable about a good Samaritan

LUK 10:30–10:37 For the Jews, the concept of a “good” Samaritan would have seemed very strange; but Jesus Christ shows that love and faith is not restricted to Israel's boundaries.

Samaria and the ministry of the early first Christians

The risen Christ includes Samaria in the church's mission

ACT 1:8

The church takes the gospel to Samaria

ACT 8:1 See also ACT 8:4–8:13, ACT 8:25

The apostles pray for the gift of the Spirit for the Samaritan converts

ACT 8:14–8:17 The delay in their reception of the Spirit until the apostles came from Jerusalem may have been God's way of removing the old Jewish-Samaritan divide in the infant church.

The Samaritan church grows

ACT 9:31

title: 7565: Sanhedrin
content:
The Hebrew form of the Greek word “synedrion”, a council. The high court of the Jews, presided over by the high priest. Its 71 members included chief priests, elders and teachers of the law.

Jesus Christ and the Sanhedrin

Herod called together the Sanhedrin on hearing of the birth of Jesus Christ

MAT 2:4 This is probably a reference to the entire Sanhedrin.

Jesus Christ acknowledged the authority of the Sanhedrin

MAT 5:22

Jesus Christ foretold that he would suffer at the hands of the Sanhedrin

MAT 16:21 parallels MRK 8:31, LUK 9:22 See also MAT 20:18 parallel MRK 10:33

These prophecies were fulfilled

MAT 26:57–26:68 parallel MRK 14:58–14:65

The Sanhedrin plotted to kill Jesus Christ after he had raised Lazarus

JHN 11:47–11:53

The Sanhedrin, powerless to execute anyone, brought Jesus Christ to Pilate

JHN 18:28–18:31

Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea were members of the Sanhedrin

See also JHN 3:1; MRK 15:43 parallel LUK 23:50–23:52

The term Sanhedrin could also apply to local councils

MAT 10:17 parallel MRK 13:9

Peter and John were brought before the Sanhedrin

ACT 4:5–4:21 See also ACT 5:21, ACT 5:27, ACT 5:34, ACT 5:41

Stephen appeared before the Sanhedrin

ACT 6:12, ACT 6:15

Stephen angered the Sanhedrin by his defence

ACT 7:54, ACT 7:57–7:58

Paul and the Sanhedrin

Paul was commissioned by the chief priests to persecute the church

ACT 9:14 Ac 22:5 makes it clear that the entire Sanhedrin was involved. See also ACT 9:1–9:2, ACT 9:21, ACT 26:10–26:12

Paul was brought before the Sanhedrin

ACT 22:30–23:1 See also ACT 23:6, ACT 23:28, ACT 24:20

Conspirators plotted to bring Paul before the Sanhedrin again, but he escaped

ACT 23:12–23:15, ACT 23:20

The Sanhedrin brought Paul to the Roman governor, Festus

ACT 25:1–25:2, ACT 25:15

title: 7570: sects
content:
The Pharisees described as a sect

ACT 26:5 The word “sect” in the NT does not have the same negative connotations as in modern society. The Greek word for “sect” is also translated as “party” in the NIV. See also LUK 5:30; ACT 15:5

The Sadducees described as a sect

ACT 5:17

Christians described as a sect

ACT 24:14 See also ACT 24:5, ACT 28:21–28:22

Sectarianism condemned

1CO 3:3–3:4; ; 2CO 12:20; ; GAL 5:20

title: 7600: History of God's people in NT
content:
• 7610: Council of Jerusalem
  • 7620: disciples
  • 7630: Twelve, the

title: 7610: Council of Jerusalem
content:
The Council of Jerusalem was possibly the first ``assembly'' of the Christian church, held c. A.D. 49

The Council met to consider whether circumcision was necessary for salvation

ACT 15:1 See also ROM 4:9; GAL 5:2–5:3

Paul and Barnabas came from Antioch to Jerusalem to meet with the apostles and elders

ACT 15:2–15:12

The Council agreed not to make it difficult for Gentiles who turned to God

ACT 15:19–15:21 The Council was concerned to avoid forcing Gentiles to adopt Jewish customs (such as circumcision), and at the same time to require Gentiles to behave considerately towards their “weaker brothers” of Jewish birth, not all of whom could be expected to acquire immediately the emancipated outlook on food-laws and the like that Peter and Paul had.

James showed how the decision had confirmed an earlier prophecy

See also ACT 15:15–15:18; AMO 9:11–9:12

The written judgment was taken to the Gentile believers by Paul, Barnabas, Silas and Judas

ACT 15:23–15:29, ACT 16:4

The Council's judgment proved to be a great encouragement to the Gentile believers

ACT 15:30–15:31, ACT 16:5

title: 7620: disciples
content: The group of people called to follow and serve Jesus Christ.

title: 7621: disciples, calling of
content:
The term is applied to Christians in general, not to the Twelve only. It emphasises the importance of Jesus Christ's followers learning from Christ as their teacher.

Disciples learning from their master

ISA 8:16; AMO 7:14 See also 1KI 20:35; NEH 3:8; ISA 19:11; MAT 10:24; MRK 2:18 parallel LUK 5:33; LUK 6:40; JHN 1:35, JHN 9:28

Those referred to as disciples in the NT

The disciples as the apostles

MAT 10:1–10:2 The apostles were uniquely appointed as founding pillars of the church, but in some respects they were also representative disciples.

The disciples as believers in general

MAT 28:19; ACT 19:1–19:2 Christian believers, not fully mature. See also LUK 6:13, LUK 6:17; ACT 6:2, ACT 11:26

The disciples as an intimate circle of believers

MAT 18:1; ; MRK 4:10

How disciples are called

It sometimes implies physically following Jesus Christ and leaving everything

MAT 4:19 parallel MRK 1:16–1:20; ; MAT 8:18–8:22 parallel LUK 9:57–9:60; ; MAT 9:9 parallel MRK 2:14

Calling in a more general spiritual sense

JHN 21:19 See also LUK 9:23, LUK 14:25–14:27; JHN 8:12, JHN 12:26, JHN 21:22

How disciples learn from Jesus Christ the teacher

By repeating the words of Jesus Christ

LUK 11:1–11:4 The rhythmical form of much of Jesus Christ's teaching suggests he intended his words to be remembered verbatim. See also MAT 5:3–5:10

By signs

JHN 2:11 See also MRK 5:37, MRK 5:40 parallel LUK 8:51

By understanding the OT Scriptures

JHN 1:45 See also LUK 18:31–18:33, LUK 24:27, LUK 24:32, LUK 24:44–24:45

By understanding the parables

MRK 4:11–4:12 parallels MAT 13:10–13:11, LUK 8:9–8:10 See also MRK 4:34

By practical experience of mission

LUK 10:23–10:24 These words follow the sending out of the seventy-two. See also MRK 6:6–6:11; LUK 9:1–9:2 parallel MAT 10:1

In periods of being alone with Jesus Christ

MRK 6:31 See also MAT 16:21

In spite of their faulty understanding

LUK 24:25 See also MRK 9:32 parallel LUK 9:45; LUK 18:34

resource.label: 2363: Jesus Christ, preaching and teaching of

title: 7622: disciples, characteristics of
content:
The content of discipleship is most clearly seen in the Christian's relationship to Jesus Christ. But this also affects a person's relationship to God the Father, the cross, the church and the world.

Disciples and their relationship to Jesus Christ

Coming to Christ and following him

MAT 4:19 See also MAT 11:28; MRK 10:21; LUK 18:16

Receiving him

REV 3:20 See also JHN 1:12

Learning from him

MAT 11:29 See also MAT 24:32 parallel MRK 13:28

Lovingly obeying him

JHN 14:15

Trusting him

JHN 14:1

Confessing him

MAT 10:38 parallel LUK 14:27 See also MRK 8:38 parallel LUK 9:26

Imitating him

1CO 11:1; ; 1PE 2:21

Reflecting him

MAT 5:16

Abiding in him

JHN 15:4 See also JHN 6:66–6:68

Disciples and their relationship to God the Father

True sons and daughters of God

MAT 5:44–5:45

Discipleship and glorifying God

See also MAT 5:16

Disciples and their relationship to the cross

Self-denial

MAT 16:24 parallels MRK 8:34, LUK 9:23

Disciples and their relationship to the church

Treating other Christians as family

MRK 3:34–3:35 See also MAT 18:15–18:17; JHN 15:12, JHN 15:17

Disciples and their relationship to the world

They are to be active in the world but are to be distinct from it

MAT 5:13 See also MAT 5:14–5:16; JHN 17:11–17:23

They are to make other disciples

MAT 28:19 See also ACT 1:8

title: 7630: Twelve, the
content: The inner circle of the twelve disciples, called by Jesus Christ to follow him and carry on his mission after his death and resurrection.

title: 7631: Twelve, calling of
content:
Jesus Christ's chosen companions, whom he called and trained to carry on his mission after his death and resurrection.

The calling of the Twelve

Their names

MAT 10:1–10:4 parallel MRK 3:14–3:19 See also LUK 6:12–6:16; ACT 1:12–1:13

Their selection was preceded by prayer

LUK 6:12–6:13

As Jesus Christ's companions, they were to share in his mission

MRK 3:14–3:15, MRK 6:7–6:13 parallels MAT 10:1, MAT 10:9–10:14, LUK 9:1–9:6; ; LUK 8:1

Jesus Christ promised them authority at the final judgment

MAT 19:28 parallel LUK 22:30

After Judas' death the Twelve became the Eleven

MAT 28:16; ; MRK 16:14; ; LUK 24:9, LUK 24:33; ; ACT 2:14

The Eleven became the Twelve again with the election of Matthias

ACT 1:26

The ministry of the Twelve after the resurrection supplemented by the appointment of deacons

ACT 6:2–6:3

The symbolic significance of the Twelve

The twelve tribes of Israel

GEN 49:28; ; EZK 47:13; ; MAT 19:28 parallel LUK 22:30 Twelve symbolises completeness and is associated with the rule of God.

title: 7632: Twelve, diverse characters of
content:
The Twelve were men of varied background, gifts and character.

The varied backgrounds of the Twelve

Peter, Andrew, James and John were fishermen: MAT 4:18, MAT 4:21; MAT 9:9 Matthew was a tax collector; ; MAT 10:4 Simon was a political activist.

The varied roles of the Twelve

Some took a prominent role in the group

GAL 2:9

Some played minor but significant roles

See also JHN 1:40–1:42 Andrew brought Peter to Jesus Christ; JHN 1:45–1:46 Philip brought Nathanael; JHN 12:20–12:22 Philip and Andrew brought the Greeks to Jesus Christ.

The varied temperaments of the Twelve

MAT 14:28 Peter was impetuous; ; LUK 9:54 James and John were fiery; ; LUK 22:4 Judas Iscariot was a traitorous loner; ; JHN 20:25 Thomas was cautious.

The Twelve had a common call to discipleship and witness

ACT 1:21–1:22

The fallibility of the Twelve

MRK 8:31–8:33 Peter misunderstood; ; MRK 16:14 Jesus Christ rebuked the Eleven for not believing reports of his resurrection; ; LUK 9:12–9:13 Jesus Christ tested their reaction to human need; ; JHN 14:8–14:9 Philip asked the way to the Father.

Jesus Christ gave the Twelve special teaching

MRK 4:10–4:11 about his parables; ; MRK 9:35 about power and position; ; LUK 18:31 about his destiny

Jesus Christ spent his last days and hours with the Twelve

MRK 11:11 after the triumphal entry into Jerusalem; ; MRK 14:17 at the Last Supper

The Twelve failed Jesus Christ

Jesus Christ's concern that they would leave him

JHN 6:67–6:70

Judas betrayed him

MAT 26:14–26:16 parallels MRK 14:10, LUK 22:3–22:4

They failed to support him in his final agony of prayer

MAT 26:40–26:41 parallels MRK 14:37–14:38, LUK 22:45–22:46

Peter disowned Jesus Christ

MAT 26:74–26:75 parallels MRK 14:71–14:72, LUK 22:60–22:61

All abandoned Jesus Christ

MAT 26:56 the exception was “the disciple whom Jesus loved”, present at the crucifixion (Jn 19:25–27)

content:
